RBH CMR Guide
Cardiovascular MRI · Royal Brompton Hospital
☀️
About this app

RBH CMR Guide is a clinical reference tool for cardiovascular MRI at Royal Brompton Hospital. It covers protocol selection, sequence acquisition, normal values, and diagnostic guidelines for use by the CMR team.

This app is intended for internal clinical reference only. All parameters should be verified against current local and national guidelines. Not for direct patient care decisions without clinical judgement.

Author
NameDr Zakariye Ashkir MBChB MRCP DPhil
RoleCardiology trainee, Royal Brompton Hospital
InstitutionRoyal Brompton Hospital, Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust, London
Royal Brompton Hospital — CMR Unit

The Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance (CMR) Unit at Royal Brompton Hospital was established in 1984 — one of the first dedicated cardiac MRI centres in the world. It runs one of the largest cardiovascular CMR services globally, performing over 20,000 scans per year across the Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust partnership.

Sources: rbht.nhs.uk · rbh-cmr.org · SCMR centre listing · Imperial College London news

References & acknowledgements
  • Kawel-Boehm N et al. Normal values for cardiovascular magnetic resonance. JCMR 2020
  • Ferreira VM et al. Cardiovascular magnetic resonance in nonischemic myocardial inflammation — LLC 2018. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2018
  • Chareonthaitawee P et al. FDG-PET/CT in cardiac sarcoidosis. JACC 2017
  • Dorbala S et al. Multimodality imaging in cardiac amyloidosis. JACC 2021
  • Indik JH et al. HRS expert consensus on MRI and CIED. Heart Rhythm 2017
  • Motwani M et al. MR of cardiac tumours and masses. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2019
  • ESC/EACVI CMR Pocket Guide, 2nd edition, 2017

For internal clinical use only · Royal Brompton Hospital · Created by Dr. Zakariye Ashkir

Version 1.0.0 · March 2026

Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Standard SA cine stack from mitral valve plane to apex
  • Include 2CH, 3CH and 4CH long-axis cines
  • Ensure temporal resolution ≤45ms for accurate EF
  • Contour both endocardial and epicardial borders for mass measurement
  • RV volumes: use transaxial stack for more reproducible results
  • Index all volumes and mass to BSA
Clinical notes & classification

Basic volumetric study to assess biventricular volumes, ejection fraction and mass. No contrast required. Normal LV EF ≥55%. RV EF ≥50%. Use age- and sex-specific normal ranges (Maceira et al). Report EDVi, ESVi, SVi, EF and massi separately for LV and RV.

  • LV EF <50% = impaired, <35% = severely impaired
  • RV EF <45% = impaired
  • Report all volumes indexed to BSA
  • Ensure consistent contouring method for serial comparisons
DCM
Cardiomyopathy Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • SA cine stack is critical — document extent of wall motion abnormality
  • LGE acquired ≥10 min post-contrast — use Look-Locker TI scout
  • Acquire LGE in 4CH, 2CH, 3CH and full SA stack
  • If T1 mapping available: acquire native T1 map pre-contrast for diffuse fibrosis assessment
  • ECV mapping (if available) provides quantitative fibrosis burden
  • Breathhold compensation (MOCO) LGE improves image quality in poor breath-holders
Clinical notes & classification

Midwall LGE is present in ~30% of DCM and is an independent predictor of sudden cardiac death and all-cause mortality. LV EF <35% + midwall LGE = high SCD risk — inform referring team. Ischaemic (subendocardial/transmural) vs non-ischaemic (midwall) LGE pattern is the key discriminator from ischaemic cardiomyopathy. Most DCM has no LGE — elevated native T1 and ECV identify diffuse interstitial fibrosis in LGE-negative DCM.

  • Midwall LGE in ~30% DCM — independent SCD predictor
  • LV EF <35% + LGE = consider ICD referral
  • Ischaemic LGE pattern → reconsider diagnosis (unrecognised CAD?)
  • T1/ECV for diffuse fibrosis in LGE-negative cases
A: Mid-ventricular SA — dilated LV with thin walls and globally impaired systolic function. B: Native T1 map — globally elevated T1 (red/orange) consistent with diffuse myocardial fibrosis. C: 4-chamber LGE — midwall fibrosis in the lateral wall (linear enhancement, subendocardium spared). D: 3-chamber LGE — confirms septal midwall LGE pattern. Midwall LGE is present in ~30% of DCM and is an independent predictor of SCD.
HCM
Cardiomyopathy Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.LVOT Cine & Flow→
5.T1 Mapping→
6.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • LVOT cines in 2 orthogonal planes — essential for SAM and LVOT gradient
  • Carefully measure maximum wall thickness in all segments
  • Include LVOT velocity mapping — VENC 2–4 m/s depending on suspected gradient
  • LGE: focus on RV insertion points (common site of HCM LGE) and hypertrophied segments
  • T1 mapping: native T1 for diffuse fibrosis beyond visible LGE
  • Assess mitral valve for SAM and coaptation defect on cine images
Clinical notes & classification

HCM criteria: wall ≥15mm (≥13mm familial HCM; ≥20mm in Black/Afro-Caribbean patients) without other cause. Septal:lateral ratio >1.3, or apical:basal ≥1.3–1.5 in apical HCM. LVOT obstruction (gradient >30 mmHg) present in ~70%. Fibrosis burden (LGE >15% LV mass) is the strongest imaging predictor of SCD risk and guides ICD decision. LGE at RV insertion points is non-specific and commonly seen in normal subjects.

  • Wall ≥15mm (≥13mm familial, ≥20mm Black patients) = HCM diagnostic criterion
  • LGE >15% LV mass = high SCD risk → ICD referral consideration
  • LVOT gradient >30 mmHg at rest = obstruction; >50 mmHg = haemodynamically significant
  • SAM of MV → eccentric mitral regurgitation
ACM / ARVC
Cardiomyopathy Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.Anatomical Stack→
5.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Transaxial RV cine stack (diaphragm to PA bifurcation) — essential for reproducible RV volumes
  • RV long-axis views: RV 2CH and RV in-/outflow cines
  • Slice thickness 6–8mm, NO inter-slice gap for RV cines
  • T1w axial black blood optional — for fatty infiltration (fat-sat comparison)
  • LGE with TI nulled for RV myocardium (separate from LV nulling)
  • Measure RVEDV/BSA and RVEF — key Task Force criteria
Clinical notes & classification

Diagnosis cannot be based on imaging alone — 2010 Modified Task Force Criteria (6 categories) required. CMR contributes to category 1 (structural). Major: regional RV WMA + RVEDV/BSA ≥110 mL/m² (M) or ≥100 mL/m² (F), or RVEF ≤40%. Minor: RVEDV/BSA 100–<110 (M) or 90–<100 (F), or RVEF >40–45%. Definite: 2 major OR 1 major+2 minor OR 4 minor criteria. RV wall motion abnormality at moderator band insertion is a common normal variant — do not overcall.

  • Diagnosis requires multiparametric TF criteria — not imaging alone
  • Major CMR criterion: RVEDV/BSA ≥110 mL/m² (M)/≥100 (F) or RVEF ≤40%
  • Minor CMR criterion: RVEDV/BSA 100–<110 (M)/90–<100 (F) or RVEF >40–45%
  • Moderator band WMA = normal variant — do not overcall
Mid-ventricular SA cine in ACM/ARVC. Yellow arrows: RV free wall dyskinesia/scalloping along the anterior RV wall — a major structural criterion. Red arrow: moderator band insertion — a common normal variant that should not be over-called as pathological. Assess RVOT separately; document any regional WMA with associated RVEDV/BSA for Task Force criteria.
Amyloid
Cardiomyopathy Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.T1 Mapping→
5.T2 Mapping→
6.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
7.ECV Mapping→
Acquisition recommendations
  • LGE: difficulty nulling myocardium is a diagnostic sign — document and photograph Look-Locker images
  • Cross-check skeletal muscle signal when choosing optimal inversion time
  • T1 mapping: native T1 is diffusely elevated — acquire in 3 SA slices if possible
  • ECV mapping essential: requires pre and post-contrast T1 maps + haematocrit from FBC
  • Check atrial septal thickness (>6mm in <20% of amyloid cases)
  • Document pericardial and pleural effusions
Clinical notes & classification

Global LGE with inability to null myocardium despite correct TI technique is the hallmark. Differentiate AL from ATTR amyloid: AL = global subendocardial LGE, LV mass <100 g/m², QALE score <13, native T1 higher than ATTR; ATTR = more diffuse/transmural LGE, LV mass >100 g/m², QALE ≥13, higher ECV. ECV >40% in both. Despite less LGE, AL has worse prognosis. Novel TTR-targeted therapy available for ATTR. Restrictive pattern: non-dilated ventricles, preserved EF (initially), dilated atria.

  • Cannot null myocardium on LGE = hallmark sign
  • AL: QALE <13, LV mass <100 g/m², subendocardial LGE
  • ATTR: QALE ≥13, LV mass >100 g/m², transmural LGE, higher ECV
  • ECV >40% in both — ATTR typically higher. Critical to differentiate: treatments differ fundamentally
Native T1 maps in cardiac amyloid. Left (4CH): markedly elevated native T1 throughout LV myocardium and RV wall (warm colours). Right (SA): uniformly elevated T1 in a concentric pattern — characteristic of infiltrative amyloid. Use QALE (T1 blood ÷ T1 myocardium) to differentiate AL (<13) from ATTR (≥13).
Myocarditis / Inflammatory CM
Inflammation Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.STIR (T2-weighted Oedema)→
5.T1 Mapping→
6.T2 Mapping→
7.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • STIR and T2 mapping MUST be acquired before contrast administration
  • T1 mapping: native T1 for oedema/inflammation; post-contrast T1 for ECV
  • LGE: classic pattern is subepicardial/midwall inferolateral wall — ensure full SA stack acquired
  • Concomitant pericardial LGE is common — acquire pericardium-focused views
  • If one LLC criterion present, recommend repeat CMR in 1–2 weeks
  • Assess RV function — RV dysfunction is strongest predictor of adverse outcome
Clinical notes & classification

Updated Lake Louise Criteria (2018) — ≥2 of: (1) T2-based oedema (T2 mapping↑ or STIR SI ratio ≥2.0); (2) T1-based injury (T1 mapping/ECV↑ or ≥1 focal non-ischaemic LGE lesion, subepicardial/midwall, subendocardium spared). Supportive: LV dysfunction or pericardial effusion. Classic LGE: subepicardial inferolateral wall. Repeat scan in 1–2 weeks if 1 criterion present or strong clinical suspicion. RV dysfunction = greatest predictor of mortality and transplantation.

  • LLC 2018: T2 oedema criterion + T1 injury criterion = diagnosis
  • LGE: subepicardial/midwall, inferolateral — subendocardium spared (≠ ischaemic)
  • Repeat CMR 1–2 weeks if only 1 criterion present
  • RV dysfunction = strongest prognostic predictor
Aorta
Aortic No contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.AV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
5.3D FISP / Gd Angiography→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Measure aortic dimensions in end-diastole from cine images (not MRA)
  • Measure at: annulus, sinuses of Valsalva, sinotubular junction, mid-ascending Ao at PA level
  • Use same slice thickness (<7mm) for all aortic cine measurements
  • Candy-cane oblique sagittal cine for aortic arch visualisation
  • AV stack for valve morphology (bicuspid? cusp prolapse?)
  • 3D FISP for full aortic dimensions and branch vessels
  • LGE if arteritis suspected
Clinical notes & classification

CMR is method of choice for non-acute aortic diseases. Surgical thresholds: root >45mm (male), >40mm (female); adjust for connective tissue disease (Marfan: >40–45mm), bicuspid AV (lower threshold). AR RegF >33% = surgical referral threshold. Aortic dissection: true lumen pulsates on cine; false lumen is larger and may thrombose. MRA overestimates aortic dimensions vs end-diastolic cine measurements.

  • Measure in end-diastole — MRA averages pulsating dimensions (overestimates)
  • Surgical threshold: root >45mm (M), >40mm (F); lower in Marfan/bicuspid AV
  • AR RegF >33% = symptom progression threshold
  • Candy-cane cine: true lumen pulsates; false lumen larger, often partially thrombosed
Shunt
Congenital No contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.Anatomical Stack→
5.AV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
6.PV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
7.Atrial Stack→
Acquisition recommendations
  • AV flow (Qs) + PV flow (Qp) for Qp:Qs calculation
  • Atrial stack to localise defect and assess rim for closure planning
  • Check adequate rim (>5mm) around defect — inferior rim most critical for closure device
  • Assess for PAPVD if Qp:Qs unexpectedly elevated
  • Look for associated Ebstein anomaly (frequently associated with ASD)
  • Measure RV and LV volumes — RVEDVI >160 mL/m² = significant volume overload
Clinical notes & classification

Qp:Qs = pulmonary flow / systemic flow (normal = 1.0). Qp:Qs >1.5 = haemodynamically significant. Qp:Qs >1.8 = red flag (consider closure). Red flags for closure: significant RV dilatation/dysfunction, Qp:Qs >1.8, pulmonary hypertension. Check for adequate device rim (especially inferior rim). Rule out PAPVD as alternative explanation for elevated Qp:Qs.

  • Qp:Qs = PV flow / AV flow
  • Qp:Qs >1.5 = haemodynamically significant; >1.8 = red flag
  • Check rim adequacy for percutaneous closure planning
  • Rule out PAPVD if Qp:Qs unexpectedly elevated
Pulmonary hypertension
Congenital No contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.AV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
5.PV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
6.Atrial Stack→
7.3D FISP / Gd Angiography→
Acquisition recommendations
  • RV transaxial cine stack essential for accurate RV volumes
  • RVOT and PA cines for MPA/LPA/RPA diameters
  • Phase-contrast AV flow + PV flow for Qp:Qs and PR fraction
  • 3D FISP/MRA for PA tree anatomy, stenoses, and branch vessel assessment
  • LV and RV function: document septal D-shaping (systolic = pressure, diastolic = volume overload)
  • Assess interventricular septal position throughout cardiac cycle
Clinical notes & classification

RV pressure overload causes systolic D-shaping of septum (pressure >volume). Dilated MPA: MPA diameter >Ao diameter suggests elevated PA pressure (Ao > MPA is normal). PA classification: left heart disease, chronic thromboembolic PH, lung disease/hypoxia, pulmonary arterial hypertension, or other causes. Fibrosis at RV insertion points in severe chronic PH. RV hypertrophy (>5mm free wall) = pressure overload.

  • Systolic D-shaping = pressure overload (PH); diastolic D-shaping = volume overload
  • MPA > Ao diameter suggests PH (Ao > MPA is normal)
  • RV free wall >5mm = hypertrophy (pressure overload)
  • Document PA anatomy for potential intervention (PTE/BPA)
Short-axis cine in pressure-overloaded RV (pulmonary hypertension). Left (systole): D-shaped LV with flattened septum in systole — hallmark of RV pressure overload. Right (diastole): septum returns toward normal curvature in diastole. Systolic D-shaping distinguishes pressure overload (PH) from volume overload, where D-shaping persists in diastole.
Acute MI
Ischaemia Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.STIR (T2-weighted Oedema)→
5.T2* Mapping (Iron)→
6.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
7.LGE — Black Blood→
Acquisition recommendations
  • STIR acquired BEFORE contrast — essential for area at risk
  • EGE (1–3 min post-contrast, TI >400ms): best sequence for MVO detection
  • LGE (≥10 min): transmurality assessment
  • Black blood LGE for subendocardial scar delineation
  • T2* for haemorrhagic infarction detection (dark core in infarct zone)
  • Assess for LV thrombus on EGE images (no enhancement mass in akinetic territory)
  • Compare LGE with cine images to differentiate blood pool from endocardial enhancement
Clinical notes & classification

T2 oedema (STIR) = area at risk. LGE scar transmurality: ≤25% = likely recovery post-revascularisation; >75% = unlikely. Myocardial salvage index = (area at risk − infarct size) / area at risk. MVO (no-reflow) = high-risk feature, equates to angiographic no-reflow, best seen on EGE. Haemorrhagic MI: T2*↓ in infarct core. LV thrombus: non-enhancing mass on EGE, in akinetic/dyskinetic territory. RV infarction: occurs in up to 50% of inferior MI.

  • MVO (no-reflow) = high-risk feature — document on EGE
  • LGE ≤25% transmural = likely functional recovery
  • LGE >75% transmural = unlikely to recover
  • Haemorrhagic MI: T2* dark core. LV thrombus: non-enhancing on EGE
Ischaemia
Ischaemia Contrast Pharmacological stress
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.Stress Perfusion→
5.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
6.LGE — Black Blood→
7.Rest Perfusion→
8.ECV Mapping→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Caffeine-free for 24 hours before examination
  • Dummy perfusion scan to check positioning and artefacts before stress
  • Adenosine 140 mcg/kg/min × ≥3 min; consider 170–210 if inadequate HR response
  • Splenic switch-off = adequate adenosine effect (not seen with regadenoson)
  • Breathhold for perfusion begins before contrast reaches LV cavity
  • Report: LV/RV volumes, 17-segment WMA, scar transmurality, stress defect extent, and correlation between scar and perfusion defect
  • Comment on suitability for revascularisation based on ischaemia burden and viability
Clinical notes & classification

Adenosine contraindications: 2nd/3rd AV block, severe reversible airways disease, known hypersensitivity. Caffeine inhibits adenosine effect. Stress defect + no LGE = inducible ischaemia (consider hibernation if WMA present). Stress defect matching LGE = fixed scar (no residual ischaemia). Normal stress + LGE = old infarct. ECV↑ in infarct zone; low/absent ECV in MVO. Prognostically significant ischaemia: ≥10% myocardium (approximately ≥2/17 segments).

  • Contraindications: 2nd/3rd AV block, severe airways disease, known hypersensitivity
  • Stress defect + no LGE = viable ischaemia → revascularisation benefit
  • Stress defect = LGE = fixed scar → no further ischaemia
  • Caffeine-free 24h; check splenic switch-off for adenosine efficacy
First-pass perfusion images (basal, mid, apical SA slices). Top row (Stress): subendocardial hypoperfusion in the inferior and inferolateral walls during adenosine stress — dark rim visible on basal and mid slices. Bottom row (Rest): perfusion normalises at rest, confirming inducible ischaemia without fixed scar. Prognostically significant if ≥2 segments (≥10% myocardium) are involved.
Viability
Ischaemia Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
5.LGE — Black Blood→
Acquisition recommendations
  • LGE acquired in all standard planes — full SA stack essential
  • Black blood LGE for precise subendocardial transmurality assessment
  • Measure transmural extent in each dysfunctional segment: ≤25%, 26–50%, 51–75%, >75%
  • Correlate LGE transmurality with wall motion on cine — akinetic + <50% LGE = hibernation
  • Consider dobutamine stress for borderline cases (26–75% transmurality)
Clinical notes & classification

LGE transmurality predicts likelihood of contractile recovery post-revascularisation. ≤25% transmural: high likelihood of recovery. 26–50%: intermediate. 51–75%: less likely. >75%: unlikely to recover — question value of revascularisation. Akinetic + <50% LGE = likely hibernating myocardium. Black blood LGE improves detection of thin subendocardial scar.

  • ≤25% transmural LGE: likely recovery post-revascularisation
  • 26–75%: intermediate — consider dobutamine stress
  • ≥75% transmural: unlikely to recover
  • Correlate WMA on cine with LGE extent in same segment
Valve disease
Valvular No contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.AV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
5.PV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Slice thickness 5mm for valve cines — consider overlapping
  • Acquire valve cines in multiple planes through leaflet tips for planimetry
  • VENC set within 25% of estimated peak velocity to avoid aliasing
  • Phase-contrast plane perpendicular to flow, at leaflet tip level
  • For MR: RegV = LV SV – forward flow above AV. RegF = RegV/LV SV × 100
  • Visual assessment alone is NOT recommended — always quantify with phase-contrast
Clinical notes & classification

AR: RegF >33% predicts symptom progression — surgical referral threshold. MR: RegV >55mL and RegF >40% predict symptom progression and are indications for surgery. Valve area by planimetry (stenosis) requires correct plane at leaflet tips — not perpendicular to annulus. MV pressure gradients are unreliable due to MV apparatus motion. CMR is the reference standard for regurgitant fraction quantification.

  • AR: RegF >33% = surgical threshold
  • MR: RegV >55mL or RegF >40% = surgical consideration
  • Visual cine assessment alone NOT recommended for stenosis/regurgitation severity
  • Planimetry: ensure imaging plane at leaflet tips
Cardiac mass
Mass Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.Anatomical Stack→
5.STIR (T2-weighted Oedema)→
6.T1 Mapping→
7.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
8.Rest Perfusion→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquire T1w and T2w images in at least 2 optimised orthogonal planes through the mass
  • Fat saturation comparison: T1w ± fat suppression confirms lipomatous lesion
  • EGE (1–3 min post-contrast) for thrombus (no uptake) vs vascular mass
  • First-pass perfusion assesses vascularity of mass
  • Tagging may help confirm mass moves with myocardium vs pericardium
  • Report: location, 3D size, relation to pericardium/myocardium/valves, signal characteristics, margins, LGE
Clinical notes & classification

Cardiac metastases are up to 1000× more common than primary tumours (melanoma, thyroid, breast, renal, lung). Common benign primaries (70%): myxoma, lipoma, fibroelastoma, fibroma, rhabdomyoma. Common malignant primaries (30%): angiosarcoma (RA), rhabdomyosarcoma, mesothelioma, lymphoma. Thrombus: T1 low, T2 low, no LGE (best on EGE). Myxoma: T1 iso, T2 high, heterogeneous LGE, vascular on perfusion. CMR characterises tissue but cannot provide histopathology.

  • Metastases 1000× more common than primary tumours
  • Thrombus: no enhancement on EGE — do not miss
  • Myxoma: most common benign LA primary, heterogeneous LGE, perfusion positive
  • Angiosarcoma: most common primary malignant, in RA, heterogeneous
Hypertension
Systemic Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
5.3D FISP / Gd Angiography→
Acquisition recommendations
  • LGE for hypertensive cardiomyopathy — patchy midwall or RV hinge point pattern
  • Gd angiography: renal arteries and adrenal glands (check eGFR first)
  • Measure wall thickness — concentric LVH, max <15mm (Caucasian), <20mm (Black)
  • Assess for basal septal hypertrophy (common in elderly hypertensive patients)
  • Renal angio: both renal arteries, check for asymmetry, stenosis, FMD
  • Adrenal views for Conn's (aldosteronoma) or phaeochromocytoma
Clinical notes & classification

Hypertensive LVH: symmetric concentric pattern, max wall thickness <15mm (Caucasian), <15–20mm (Black). Basal septal hypertrophy common in elderly/hypertensive patients. LGE: occasional patchy pattern or focal midwall at RV hinge points. Renovascular hypertension: renal artery stenosis (atherosclerotic >65 years; FMD <40 years female). Always check eGFR before renal Gd angiography.

  • Hypertensive LVH: concentric, max wall <15mm (Caucasian)
  • Check eGFR before renal Gd angiography
  • Renovascular HT: consider in young female (FMD) or elderly with asymmetric kidneys
  • Adrenals: Conn's (aldosteronoma) and phaeochromocytoma are treatable causes
Pericardium
Inflammation Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.STIR (T2-weighted Oedema)→
5.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
6.Real-Time Free-Breathing Cine→
Acquisition recommendations
  • STIR before contrast for active pericardial oedema/inflammation
  • Real-time SA cine in multiple views + 4CH during normal respiration — look for septal bounce
  • High-resolution T1w transaxial for pericardial thickness measurement (normal <3mm)
  • LGE for pericardial enhancement — predicts response to anti-inflammatory therapy
  • Pericardial effusion: T1 signal characterises content (transudate vs exudate vs haemorrhage)
  • Document any RA/LA/RV/LV diastolic collapse for tamponade assessment
Clinical notes & classification

Constrictive pericarditis: pericardial thickening ≥3mm + septal bounce + preserved LV function + impaired diastolic filling. Constriction can exist with normal or patchy pericardial thickness. Real-time cine is essential — paradoxical septal motion during inspiration (septal bounce) is the key functional sign. LGE predicts response to anti-inflammatory therapy (active pericarditis). Pericardial effusion: transudate = simple signal on cine and T1; exudate/haemorrhage = complex signal.

  • Pericardial thickening ≥3mm + septal bounce = constrictive pericarditis
  • Constriction possible with normal thickness — real-time cine is essential
  • LGE pericardial enhancement predicts response to anti-inflammatory treatment
  • Haemorrhagic effusion: high T1 signal; complex cine signal
Pericardial disease — multiparametric CMR. A: HASTE transaxial — pericardial effusion (dark signal) surrounding the heart. B: Cine bSSFP — persistent effusion with pericardial thickening visible anteriorly. C: T2-weighted — large pericardial effusion with bright signal (simple transudate); note RA compression indicating haemodynamic significance. D: LGE — pericardial enhancement indicating active inflammation; LGE predicts response to anti-inflammatory therapy.
Thalassaemia
Systemic No contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.T2* Mapping (Iron)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • T2* ROI placed in the mid-ventricular septum — fewest artefacts
  • Acquire transaxial liver T2* slice simultaneously
  • Position liver slice to avoid large hepatic vessels
  • Coach patient carefully before T2* scan — relatively long breath-hold required
  • Serial T2* follow-up guides chelation therapy adjustment
  • High correlation between T2* and native T1: T2*↓ correlates with T1↓
Clinical notes & classification

T2* quantification in ventricular septum: ≥20ms = normal (low risk). 14–20ms = mild iron, intermediate risk. 10–14ms = moderate iron, significant loading — consider increasing chelation. ≤10ms = severe, high risk of cardiac failure — urgent chelation review. Also measure hepatic T2* — isolated cardiac or liver involvement is possible. Isolated cardiac T2* ≤10ms warrants urgent haematology review regardless of liver iron.

  • T2* ≥20ms = normal; <10ms = severe — urgent chelation review
  • Measure both myocardial (septum) AND hepatic T2*
  • Isolated cardiac iron loading (T2* ≤10ms) possible without significant hepatic iron
  • Serial T2* monitoring every 6–12 months guides chelation therapy
T2* septal ROI placement for iron quantification. The blue segment marks the mid-septal ROI — positioned in the interventricular septum away from epicardial fat and adjacent lung. Triangular exclusion zone avoids the RV blood pool. Use this standardised approach to ensure reproducibility for serial monitoring. T2* ≥20ms = normal; 14–20ms = mild; 10–14ms = moderate; <10ms = severe (urgent review).
Dilated RV
Congenital Contrast
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details
1.HASTE Localizer→
2.TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)→
3.Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)→
4.Anatomical Stack→
5.AV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
6.PV Flow (Phase-Contrast)→
7.Atrial Stack→
8.LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)→
Acquisition recommendations
  • Transaxial RV cine stack — most reproducible for RV volumes
  • RVOT and PA cines for MPA/PV assessment
  • PV phase-contrast for pulmonary regurgitation fraction
  • Atrial stack for ASD/PAPVD assessment
  • LGE for RV fibrosis at insertion points
  • Assess interventricular septal position: diastolic D-shaping = volume overload; systolic = pressure
Clinical notes & classification

Most common cause in adults: pulmonary regurgitation after ToF repair. PR fraction by phase-contrast is key — RVEDVI >160 mL/m² combined with PR fraction >40% = threshold for pulmonary valve replacement consideration. RV volume overload → diastolic D-shaping of septum. RV pressure overload → systolic D-shaping. RV free wall >5mm = hypertrophy (pressure overload). Fibrosis at RV insertion points on LGE.

  • Repaired ToF: RVEDVI >160 mL/m² + PR >40% → consider PVR
  • Diastolic D-shaping = volume overload (PR, TR, shunt)
  • Systolic D-shaping = pressure overload (PH, PS)
  • RV free wall >5mm = pressure-related hypertrophy
Short-axis cine in volume-overloaded RV (dilated RV / pulmonary regurgitation). Left (systole): LV septum bows toward LV in diastole — diastolic D-shaping, characteristic of RV volume overload (contrast with systolic D-shaping in PH). Right (diastole): marked septal flattening persisting in diastole confirms volume (not pressure) overload. Measure RVEDVI and PR fraction by phase-contrast to assess PVR threshold (RVEDVI >160 mL/m² + PR >40%).
ACHD
Congenital Discuss
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details

Bespoke protocol — see clinical notes below.

Acquisition recommendations
  • Discuss with congenital CMR specialist — protocol is entirely bespoke
  • Long acquisition time expected — patient preparation and consent important
  • Multiple flow measurements (AV, PV, branch PAs, vena cavae) often required
  • 3D MRA for complex anatomy (systemic and pulmonary venous connections)
Clinical notes & classification

Adult congenital heart disease requires entirely bespoke protocol planning in conjunction with the congenital CMR specialist. Specific considerations: post-Fontan (hepatic venous congestion, Glenn flows, fenestration); post-arterial switch (coronary reimplantation, neo-aortic root dilatation); complex shunts (Qp:Qs, differential PA flow); heterotaxy (atrial arrangement, vascular connections). Always coordinate with congenital cardiologist.

  • Bespoke protocol — MUST discuss with congenital CMR specialist
  • Post-Fontan: multiple flow measurements, hepatic assessment
  • Post-arterial switch: coronary origins, neo-aortic root
  • Heterotaxy: atrial arrangement, vascular connections
Paeds
Congenital Discuss
Sequences Click any sequence for technical details

Bespoke protocol — see clinical notes below.

Acquisition recommendations
  • Discuss with paediatric CMR team — weight-based dosing and protocol
  • Consider sedation or GA requirements — discuss with paediatric anaesthetics
  • Dedicated paediatric RF coil essential
  • MR scanning not established as safe for neonates <2 years — assess benefit vs risk
Clinical notes & classification

Paediatric CMR requires specialist planning by the paediatric CMR team. Weight-based gadolinium dosing. Sedation/GA often required in children <8 years. Use of dedicated paediatric coils. Protocols follow SCMR/AEPC paediatric CMR guidelines. Coordinate with paediatric cardiology, anaesthetics, and CMR team for each case.

  • Weight-based Gd dosing essential
  • Neonates <2 years: assess benefit vs risk carefully
  • Dedicated paediatric coil required
  • Coordinate cardiology + anaesthetics + CMR team
Anatomical Stack
Structural
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — balanced SSFP (bSSFP), cine mode
WeightingT2/T1-weighted (bright blood)
OrientationTailored to the clinical question: SA stack over RV (ARVC/ACM, dilated RV), transaxial through chest (shunt/PAPVD), or targeted stack over a mass
Slice thickness6–8 mm, contiguous (no gap)
AcquisitionBreath-hold per slice; navigator-gated if poor breath-holder
CoverageDetermined by region of interest — typically apex to pulmonary artery bifurcation for RV; full thorax for shunt assessment
Acquisition recommendations
  • ACM / ARVC: Acquire SA stack from RV apex to pulmonary valve — systematic slice-by-slice review of RV free wall for wall motion abnormalities, aneurysms and dyskinetic segments. Extend into RVOT. Use real-time cine if arrhythmia is present
  • Shunt (ASD/VSD): Transaxial stack from diaphragm to above pulmonary veins — assess for partial anomalous pulmonary venous drainage (PAPVD) by tracing each pulmonary vein to its draining chamber. Also evaluate ASD rim anatomy and adjacent structures
  • Dilated RV: SA stack from apex to pulmonary bifurcation — evaluate RV free wall thickness and regional wall motion. Identify diastolic D-shaping of septum and assess tricuspid valve plane
  • Cardiac mass: Targeted stack in the plane best demonstrating the mass — assess extent, attachment site, mobility, and invasion of adjacent structures slice by slice. Use multiple planes if mass borders are complex
  • Coverage should extend slightly beyond the region of interest to avoid missing adjacent pathology
Indications & clinical context

Provides slice-by-slice anatomical review of a specific region that cannot be fully assessed from standard long-axis views alone. In ACM/ARVC and dilated RV, the stack enables systematic evaluation of RV free wall motion to identify regional dyskinesia and microaneurysms required for Task Force criteria. In shunt protocols, it enables complete pulmonary venous mapping to exclude PAPVD. In cardiac mass protocols, it defines the three-dimensional extent and relationships of the mass to surrounding structures.

  • ACM / ARVC
  • Dilated RV
  • Shunt
  • Cardiac Mass
Cine bSSFP (LV/RV Function)
Functional
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — balanced SSFP (TrueFISP / bFFE / FIESTA)
WeightingT2/T1-weighted (bright blood)
ViewsSA stack + 2CH, 3CH, 4CH long-axis cines (LVOT ×2 for LVOT protocols)
Slice thickness6–10 mm SA; inter-slice gap 0–4 mm to equal 10 mm total
Temporal resolution≤ 45 ms
Parallel imagingRecommended to reduce breath-hold time
RV optionTransaxial RV stack (diaphragm to PA bifurcation) — more reproducible RV volumes
Acquisition recommendations
  • Plan SA stack perpendicular to the long axis of the LV, covering mitral valve to apex
  • In a healthy heart, usually one fewer slice to contour at base in end-systole (longitudinal shortening)
  • Correlate SA to long-axis views to correctly identify atrioventricular valve plane
  • Use movie function in analysis software for correct contour alignment
  • For RV: transaxial stack is more reproducible than SA for volume measurement
  • Temporal resolution ≤45ms is essential for accurate EF measurement
  • Consider real-time imaging for patients with arrhythmia or poor breath-holding
Indications & clinical context

The gold standard sequence for biventricular volumetric assessment, providing accurate measurement of EDV, ESV, SV, EF, and myocardial mass indexed to BSA. Detects regional wall motion abnormalities and is essential for LVOT assessment in obstructive HCM. The SA stack enables systematic contouring from base to apex, while long-axis views (2CH, 3CH, 4CH) provide complementary functional and morphological assessment.

  • All CMR protocols (core functional sequence)
LVOT Cine & Flow
Flow
Technical parameters
Cine viewsGradient echo — balanced SSFP (bSSFP) cine; phase-contrast gradient echo (flow)
WeightingCine: T2/T1-weighted; flow: velocity-encoded (phase contrast)
FlowThrough-plane phase-contrast perpendicular to aortic root
VENC150–200 cm/s normal; increase if aortic stenosis/HCM obstruction suspected
Slice thickness< 7 mm for flow; 5 mm for cine
Phases20–25 phases (breath-hold); 30 phases (free-breathing)
Pixel resolutionMinimum 4–6 pixels across vessel diameter
Acquisition recommendations
  • Plan cine views from SA base and 4CH to visualise LVOT and aortic valve in full
  • LVOT cine essential for detection of systolic anterior motion (SAM) of mitral valve in HCM
  • Phase-contrast plane perpendicular to aortic root — position slice close to valve to minimise underestimation of AR
  • VENC set within 25% of true peak velocity: too low = aliasing; too high = underestimation
  • For LVOT obstruction (HCM): orthogonal views in 2 planes to document gradient
  • Coronary back-flow into aorta may overestimate aortic regurgitation — awareness essential
Indications & clinical context

Quantifies aortic forward flow, regurgitant fraction (RegF >33% = surgical threshold in aortic regurgitation), and LVOT gradient in obstructive HCM. Cine through the LVOT demonstrates systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve and dynamic obstruction. An LVOT gradient >30 mmHg at rest indicates obstruction; >50 mmHg is haemodynamically significant. Phase-contrast velocity mapping confirms the gradient non-invasively.

  • HCM
  • Aorta
  • Valve Disease
  • Pulmonary Hypertension
AV Flow (Phase-Contrast)
Flow
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — phase-contrast velocity encoding (through-plane)
WeightingVelocity-encoded (flow quantification; magnitude + phase images)
PositionPerpendicular to aortic root, just above valve leaflet tips
VENCNormal: 150 cm/s. Adjust for pathology (severe AS: >400 cm/s)
Slice thickness< 7 mm
Phases20–25 (breath-hold) / 30 (free-breathing)
Pixel resolutionMin 4–6 pixels across vessel diameter
Acquisition recommendations
  • Plan from sagittal and coronal LVOT views — perpendicular to aortic root
  • Position slice close to valve to minimise underestimation of AR
  • Adjust VENC within 25% of estimated peak velocity
  • Use orthogonal acquisition to define peak velocity in stenosis
  • Consider flow measurement in descending aorta to detect holodiastolic backflow (severe AR)
  • RegV = LV SV – forward flow. RegF (%) = RegV / LV SV × 100
Indications & clinical context

Phase-contrast flow through the aortic valve quantifies aortic forward flow, regurgitant volume, and regurgitant fraction. An aortic regurgitant fraction >33% predicts symptom progression and is used as a surgical threshold. AV forward flow combined with MPA flow provides the Qp:Qs ratio for shunt quantification. Holodiastolic flow reversal in the descending aorta is an additional marker of severe aortic regurgitation.

  • Aorta
  • Shunt
  • Pulmonary Hypertension
  • Valve Disease
  • Dilated RV
PV Flow (Phase-Contrast)
Flow
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — phase-contrast velocity encoding (through-plane)
WeightingVelocity-encoded (flow quantification; magnitude + phase images)
PositionPerpendicular to main pulmonary artery, above pulmonary valve
VENCNormal: 100 cm/s. Adjust for pulmonary stenosis/regurgitation
Slice thickness< 7 mm
Phases20–25 (breath-hold) / 30 (free-breathing)
Pixel resolutionMin 4–6 pixels across vessel diameter
Acquisition recommendations
  • Plan from sagittal and coronal RVOT views — perpendicular to MPA
  • Separate flow measurements for LPA and RPA if differential lung perfusion suspected
  • PR fraction: important in repaired ToF — RVEDVI >160 mL/m² + PR fraction >40% → consider PVR
  • Severe PR jet is laminar and difficult to visualise on cine — phase-contrast is essential
  • MPA/Ao flow ratio = Qp:Qs for shunt quantification (normal = 1.0)
  • Qp:Qs >1.5 = haemodynamically significant; >1.8 = red flag for closure
Indications & clinical context

Phase-contrast flow through the main pulmonary artery is essential for Qp:Qs shunt calculation (MPA flow / Ao flow) and for quantifying pulmonary regurgitation in patients with repaired tetralogy of Fallot. A PR fraction >40% combined with RVEDVI >160 mL/m² is a threshold for considering pulmonary valve replacement. MPA diameter exceeding aortic diameter on cine bSSFP is an indirect sign of elevated pulmonary artery pressure.

  • Shunt
  • Pulmonary Hypertension
  • Valve Disease
  • Dilated RV
STIR (T2-weighted Oedema)
Tissue Characterisation
Technical parameters
Sequence typeSpin echo — STIR (Short TI Inversion Recovery), dark blood prepared
WeightingT2-weighted with fat suppression (dark blood)
Slice thickness≥ 10 mm (to ensure adequate SNR)
TimingMUST be acquired BEFORE contrast administration
ReadoutMid-diastolic readout to reduce motion artefact
Body coilUse body coil or surface coil intensity correction algorithms
Pre-pulse thicknessDark blood pre-pulse thickness > longitudinal LV shortening
Acquisition recommendations
  • Always acquired BEFORE any contrast injection — contrast will suppress T2 signal
  • Slice thickness ≥10mm is mandatory for adequate SNR — thinner slices will be noisy
  • Dark blood pre-pulse slice thickness must be greater than LV longitudinal shortening
  • Use body coil (or intensity correction) to avoid coil-related signal differences
  • Slow flow artefacts may cause high signal at endocardial border — be aware
  • Consider T2 mapping instead of STIR for more quantitative oedema assessment
  • Normal T2 SI ratio myocardium:skeletal muscle < 2.0
Indications & clinical context

STIR detects myocardial oedema using T2-weighted contrast with fat suppression. A signal intensity ratio ≥2.0 (myocardium:skeletal muscle) indicates oedema and satisfies the T2-based criterion in the Lake Louise Criteria. In acute MI, the area of T2 oedema represents the area at risk; when combined with LGE scar extent, this allows calculation of the myocardial salvage index. STIR also differentiates acute from chronic myocardial injury — only acute lesions show oedema.

  • Myocarditis / Inflammatory CM
  • Acute MI
  • Pericardium
  • Cardiac Mass
T1 Mapping
Tissue Characterisation
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — Modified Look-Locker Inversion Recovery (MOLLI / ShMOLLI) or SASHA
WeightingT1-weighted (inversion recovery; generates quantitative T1 maps)
PlanesSingle mid-ventricular SA slice, or 3-slice "3 of 5" technique
AcquisitionOne slice per breath-hold
Post-contrastWait ≥15 min post-contrast injection for post-Gd T1
ECV calculationRequires pre- and post-contrast T1 + haematocrit from FBC
FOV/voxelMust NOT change between pre- and post-contrast acquisitions
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquire one slice per breath-hold for best image quality
  • Check scanner-generated T1 map for breathing artefact before proceeding
  • FOV and voxel size must be identical for pre and post-contrast acquisitions (ECV calculation)
  • Native T1 values vary by field strength, vendor and pulse sequence — always use site-specific reference ranges
  • 3T gives longer native T1 than 1.5T — do not compare across field strengths
  • Post-contrast T1: wait ≥15 min; consider imaging later in low output states
  • ECV = (1 – Hct) × ΔR1 myocardium / ΔR1 blood
Indications & clinical context

Native T1 mapping provides quantitative tissue characterisation without gadolinium. T1 is elevated in oedema, fibrosis, and amyloid infiltration, and reduced in iron overload and Anderson-Fabry disease (characteristically in the basal inferolateral wall). Combined with post-contrast T1 and haematocrit, it enables ECV calculation — ECV >40% is strongly suggestive of amyloid. T1 mapping is also elevated in diffuse fibrosis seen in DCM and HCM, where focal LGE may underestimate disease burden.

  • DCM
  • HCM
  • Amyloid
  • Thalassaemia
  • Myocarditis / Inflammatory CM
T2 Mapping
Tissue Characterisation
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — T2-prepared bSSFP; or spin echo-based T2 mapping (vendor-specific)
WeightingT2-weighted (generates quantitative T2 maps)
PlanesSA mid-ventricular slice or 3-slice "3 of 5" technique
TimingAcquired BEFORE contrast administration
Normal values~45–55 ms at 1.5T (site-specific ranges essential)
AcquisitionOne slice per breath-hold
Advantage over STIRQuantitative; more sensitive for oedema detection
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquired before contrast — contrast suppresses T2 signal
  • More sensitive than STIR for oedema detection and quantification
  • T2 mapping preferred over STIR in Updated Lake Louise Criteria (2018)
  • Use site-specific reference ranges — values vary by sequence and scanner
  • T2↑ indicates oedema/inflammation; confirms acute injury in myocarditis and acute MI
  • Combine with T1 mapping for multiparametric tissue characterisation
Indications & clinical context

T2 mapping provides quantitative assessment of myocardial oedema and is the preferred oedema sequence in the Updated Lake Louise Criteria (2018). T2 elevation confirms active inflammation or oedema in myocarditis, acute MI, and Tako-Tsubo cardiomyopathy. It is more sensitive than STIR for oedema detection and allows global as well as regional assessment. T2 is reduced in iron overload.

  • Myocarditis / Inflammatory CM
  • Amyloid
  • Acute MI
T2* Mapping (Iron)
Tissue Characterisation
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — multi-echo GRE (T2* decay fitting across multiple TEs)
WeightingT2*-weighted (susceptibility-sensitive; generates quantitative T2* maps)
PlanesSingle mid-ventricular SA slice; single transaxial liver slice
ROIPlaced in ventricular septum (fewer artefacts than lateral wall)
AcquisitionSingle breath-hold — coach patient carefully beforehand
NormalMyocardial T2* ≥20 ms = normal
LiverTransaxial liver slice acquired simultaneously — avoid large hepatic vessels
Acquisition recommendations
  • Ensure good breath-hold coaching before acquiring — scan duration is relatively long
  • ROI placed in ventricular septum — iron deposition similar in all LV segments; septum has fewer artefacts
  • Position transaxial liver slice carefully — avoid large hepatic vessels for accurate liver T2*
  • High correlation between myocardial T2* and native T1: T2*↓ correlates with T1↓
  • Check that the septum is of good image quality before quantification
  • Isolated cardiac or liver iron involvement is possible — measure both
Indications & clinical context

T2* is the gold standard for non-invasive quantification of myocardial iron loading in transfusion-dependent patients. T2* ≥20 ms is normal (low cardiac risk); 14–20 ms indicates mild iron loading (intermediate risk, review chelation); 10–14 ms indicates moderate loading (consider intensifying chelation); and <10 ms indicates severe loading with high risk of cardiac failure requiring urgent haematology review. T2* can also detect haemorrhagic transformation within acute myocardial infarction (dark core within the infarct zone).

  • Thalassaemia
  • Acute MI (haemorrhagic infarction assessment)
LGE — White Blood (BH MOCO)
Late Gadolinium Enhancement
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — segmented inversion recovery GRE (IR-GRE)
WeightingT1-weighted post-gadolinium (nulled normal myocardium; scar appears bright)
Views4CH, 3CH, 2CH, SA stack
In-plane resolution< 2 mm
Timing≥ 10 min post-contrast injection (standard dose)
TI settingLook-Locker / TI scout to null normal myocardium
ReadoutMid-to-late diastole; every other heartbeat (every 3rd in tachycardia)
Contrast dose0.1–0.2 mmol/kg
Acquisition recommendations
  • TI must be set to null normal myocardium — repeat Look-Locker scout every few minutes as TI changes
  • Increase TI by 10–15 ms every few minutes as contrast washes out
  • Consider imaging earlier if lower Gd doses are used; later in low output states
  • Use phase-sensitive LGE (PSIR) with fixed TI as an alternative to standard IR-GRE
  • Use saturation bands across spinal column and anterior chest wall to reduce ghosting
  • Phase-swap to confirm pathology vs artefact (real fibrosis present regardless of phase direction)
  • Always acquire orthogonal plane through suspected enhancement
  • Infiltrative disease (amyloid) suspected if normal myocardium cannot be nulled despite correct technique
Indications & clinical context

The cornerstone scar imaging sequence, used in the majority of CMR protocols. Ischaemic LGE follows a subendocardial-to-transmural pattern within a coronary territory, whereas non-ischaemic LGE is typically midwall or subepicardial with subendocardial sparing. Transmurality predicts viability: ≤25% transmural scar predicts likely functional recovery after revascularisation, while >75% predicts unlikely recovery. In amyloid, the inability to null normal myocardium despite correct technique is a hallmark diagnostic sign.

  • DCM
  • HCM
  • ACM / ARVC
  • Amyloid
  • Myocarditis
  • Acute MI
  • Ischaemia
  • Viability
  • Cardiac Mass
  • Pericardium
  • Sarcoidosis (via Myocarditis protocol)
LGE — Black Blood
Late Gadolinium Enhancement
Technical parameters
Sequence typeSpin echo / gradient echo hybrid — dark blood IR prepared (e.g. DANTE-PSIR)
WeightingT1-weighted post-gadolinium (dark blood pool; scar appears bright)
AdvantageSuppresses blood pool signal → improved subendocardial scar detection
ViewsSA stack and long-axis views
Timing≥ 10 min post-contrast
In-plane resolution< 2 mm
Use caseParticularly valuable when white blood LGE is ambiguous at subendocardial border
Acquisition recommendations
  • Used in addition to (not instead of) white blood LGE
  • Particularly useful where thin subendocardial scar may be obscured by bright blood pool on white blood LGE
  • Check for dark rim artefact — reduce contrast dose or increase in-plane spatial resolution if problematic
  • Consider for all ischaemic protocols where scar transmurality assessment is critical
  • Phase-swap technique to confirm genuine subendocardial scar vs artefact
Indications & clinical context

Black blood LGE suppresses the blood pool signal, significantly improving detection and transmurality assessment of thin subendocardial scar — particularly in the inferior and lateral walls where standard white blood LGE may be ambiguous due to adjacent bright blood. It is acquired in addition to (not instead of) white blood LGE, and is most valuable when scar transmurality determines management decisions such as revascularisation.

  • Acute MI
  • Ischaemia
  • Viability
ECV Mapping
Tissue Characterisation
Technical parameters
CalculationECV = (1 – Hct) × ΔR1 myocardium / ΔR1 blood
WeightingT1-weighted (pre- and post-contrast T1 maps combined with haematocrit)
RequiresPre-contrast T1 map + post-contrast T1 map + haematocrit (FBC)
TimingPost-contrast T1 acquired ≥15 min after injection
FOV/voxelMUST be identical for pre- and post-contrast acquisitions
Normal ECV~25–27%
InterpretationECV >40% strongly suggests amyloid infiltration
Acquisition recommendations
  • FOV and voxel size must be absolutely identical for pre- and post-contrast T1 acquisitions
  • Request haematocrit from FBC — this must be available before ECV can be calculated
  • Post-contrast T1 map acquired ≥15 min post-injection for standard dose
  • ECV mapping requires the same slice positions as pre-contrast T1 maps
  • Check for breathing artefact on both pre and post-contrast T1 maps before generating ECV map
Indications & clinical context

ECV quantifies the extracellular interstitial space, providing a direct measure of diffuse myocardial fibrosis or infiltration. ECV >40% is strongly suggestive of cardiac amyloidosis and should prompt urgent clinical correlation. ECV is elevated in diffuse interstitial fibrosis (DCM, HCM) but remains normal in areas of dense replacement fibrosis without extracellular expansion. In acute MI, low ECV in the microvascular obstruction zone reflects the inability of contrast to reach ruptured myocytes. Elevated ECV is an independent predictor of adverse outcomes.

  • Amyloid
  • Ischaemia
  • DCM
  • HCM
Stress Perfusion
Perfusion
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — saturation-recovery GRE (or hybrid GRE-EPI) with parallel imaging
WeightingT1-weighted (first-pass gadolinium uptake; signal proportional to perfusion)
PlanesSA — minimum 3 slices using "3 of 5" technique; ± long axis
In-plane resolution< 2.5 mm
Contrast0.05–0.1 mmol/kg at 3–7 mL/s + 30 mL saline flush
DurationImage for ≥40 heartbeats
Adenosine140 mcg/kg/min × ≥3 min (consider 170–210 if inadequate response)
Regadenoson0.4 mg single IV injection — no splenic switch-off
Acquisition recommendations
  • Always perform a dummy scan first to check slice positioning and artefacts
  • Caffeine-free for 24 hours prior to stress CMR
  • Contraindications: 2nd/3rd AV block, severe reversible airways disease, known hypersensitivity
  • Splenic switch-off (spleen becomes dark) confirms adequate adenosine effect — not seen with regadenoson
  • Breath-hold begins during early phases of contrast infusion, before contrast reaches LV cavity
  • Switch to alternate-heartbeat acquisition if heart rate >100 bpm
  • Dark rim artefact common — reduce contrast dose/infusion speed or increase in-plane resolution
  • Stress perfusion defect + no LGE = inducible ischaemia; defect matches LGE = scar
Indications & clinical context

Vasodilator stress perfusion identifies inducible ischaemia in suspected coronary artery disease. A stress perfusion defect without corresponding LGE indicates viable ischaemic myocardium (hibernation if wall motion abnormality is present). The combined stress/rest/LGE approach allows complete ischaemia and viability assessment in a single examination, with diagnostic accuracy comparable to invasive FFR.

  • Ischaemia
  • Cardiac Mass (rest perfusion only — for vascularity assessment)
Rest Perfusion
Perfusion
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — saturation-recovery GRE (identical to stress perfusion sequence)
WeightingT1-weighted (first-pass gadolinium; compare with stress for reversibility)
TimingAcquired after stress perfusion, following LV function cines
Contrast0.05–0.1 mmol/kg (top-up dose after stress injection)
PlanesSame SA slice positions as stress acquisition
PurposeDifferentiates fixed defect (scar) from reversible defect (ischaemia)
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquired at the same SA slice positions as stress perfusion — enables direct comparison
  • Top-up contrast dose after stress injection and function cines
  • A stress defect that normalises at rest = inducible ischaemia
  • A defect that persists at rest = fixed defect (correlate with LGE for scar)
  • Cardiac mass protocol: rest perfusion used to assess vascularity of mass (not stress)
Indications & clinical context

Rest perfusion is acquired at the same SA slice positions as stress perfusion, enabling direct comparison. A stress defect that normalises at rest indicates inducible ischaemia, while a defect persisting at rest represents a fixed defect that should be correlated with LGE for scar assessment. In cardiac mass protocols, rest perfusion (without stress) assesses mass vascularity — no perfusion in thrombus; heterogeneous perfusion in myxoma.

  • Ischaemia
  • Cardiac Mass
3D FISP / Gd Angiography
Angiography
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — 3D spoiled GRE (contrast-enhanced MRA) or 3D balanced SSFP (FISP/TrueFISP)
WeightingT1-weighted (CE-MRA); T2/T1-weighted (3D bSSFP)
Contrast0.1–0.2 mmol/kg; infusion pump with flush
VolumeLarge 3D target region covering area of interest
TimingBolus track or test bolus for arterial/venous phase timing
ResolutionSub-millimetre isotropic preferred for vessel delineation
Acquisition recommendations
  • Optimise timing: centre of k-space must be acquired at same time as contrast bolus reaches vessel of interest
  • FOV must cover all relevant anatomy including collateral or aberrant vessels
  • Perform a dummy acquisition to check positioning before contrast administration
  • MRA is usually non-ECG-triggered — pulsating aortic dimensions are averaged (not end-diastolic)
  • Use windowing carefully when measuring aortic dimensions from MRA — potential overestimation
Indications & clinical context

Delineates aortic anatomy (aneurysm, dissection, coarctation), pulmonary artery tree, and renal arteries. In hypertensive patients, identifies renal artery stenosis as a cause of renovascular hypertension and detects adrenal masses. Aortic dimensions should be measured from end-diastolic cine bSSFP rather than MRA, as MRA averages across the cardiac cycle and overestimates true luminal diameter. Always check eGFR before administering gadolinium for angiography.

  • Aorta
  • Pulmonary Hypertension
  • Hypertension
Real-Time Free-Breathing Cine
Functional
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — real-time balanced SSFP (no ECG gating, no breath-hold)
WeightingT2/T1-weighted (bright blood)
Temporal resolutionReduced vs standard cine (40–80 ms)
Spatial resolutionReduced vs standard cine
ViewsSA and 4CH for respiratory septal bounce assessment
PurposeVentricular interdependence / septal motion during respiration
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquire in multiple SA views and 4CH during normal respiration
  • Paradoxical septal motion during inspiration (septal bounce) = constrictive pericarditis
  • Septal bounce is often limited to one part of the septum — multiple SA views needed
  • Also valuable for patients with arrhythmia or who cannot breath-hold
  • Inferior temporal/spatial resolution is acceptable for this functional indication
Indications & clinical context

Detects paradoxical septal motion (septal bounce) during respiration — the key functional sign of constrictive pericarditis. Real-time cine is the only sequence that reliably demonstrates ventricular interdependence because it acquires continuously during free breathing. Also valuable in pericardial effusion to assess tamponade physiology, and as a fallback for volumetric assessment in patients with arrhythmia or inability to breath-hold.

  • Pericardium
Atrial Stack
Structural
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — balanced SSFP (bSSFP), cine mode
WeightingT2/T1-weighted (bright blood)
CoverageBoth atria, atrial septum, pulmonary veins
Slice thickness6–8 mm
PurposeLocalise septal defect; atrial volume; pulmonary vein anatomy
Acquisition recommendations
  • Position stack to cover both atria, interatrial septum and pulmonary vein ostia
  • Identify location of ASD/PFO relative to surrounding structures for closure planning
  • Assess rim around defect — adequate rim (>5mm) required for percutaneous closure (except for aortic rim)
  • Check for PAPVD (partial anomalous pulmonary venous drainage) if Qp:Qs unexpectedly elevated
  • LA and RA volume measurement uses biplane method: Vol = 0.85 × (Area 4CH × Area 2CH) / Length
Indications & clinical context

Localises atrial septal defects and assesses suitability for percutaneous closure by evaluating surrounding rims. Identifies partial anomalous pulmonary venous drainage (PAPVD) which may explain an unexpectedly elevated Qp:Qs. Also used for LA and RA volume measurement (LA volume/BSA >53 mL/m² indicates significant enlargement).

  • Shunt
  • Pulmonary Hypertension
  • Dilated RV
HASTE Localizer
Structural & Functional
Technical parameters
Sequence typeSpin echo — Half-Fourier single-shot Turbo Spin Echo (HASTE)
WeightingT2-weighted (dark blood)
PlanesTransaxial, coronal, and sagittal
Slice thickness8–10 mm
AcquisitionFree-breathing single-shot — very fast, robust in arrhythmia and poor breath-holders
CoverageEntire thorax from diaphragm to above aortic arch
ContrastDark blood — myocardium grey, blood dark, fat bright
ECG gatingMid-diastolic trigger preferred; ungated acceptable for initial survey
Acquisition recommendations
  • Always the first sequence acquired at the start of every CMR study — before any cines or contrast
  • Acquire all 3 planes (transaxial, coronal, sagittal) for a full structural thoracic survey
  • Confirm patient is at isocentre and coil elements provide adequate signal before proceeding
  • Transaxial HASTE is the primary planning reference for all subsequent cine views
  • Review before proceeding: identify anatomical variants, pericardial effusion, mediastinal pathology, pleural effusions
Indications & clinical context

HASTE provides a structural survey of the thorax using T2-weighted dark blood contrast, allowing identification of pericardial thickening or effusion, mediastinal masses, pleural effusions, and anatomical variants. The transaxial stack is the primary reference for planning the SA cine stack and long-axis views. It is also the key sequence for reviewing extracardiac findings.

  • All CMR protocols (mandatory first localizer)
TruFi Localizer (bSSFP Scout)
Structural & Functional
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — balanced SSFP (TrueFISP / bFFE / FIESTA)
WeightingT2/T1-weighted (bright blood)
PlanesTransaxial, coronal, and sagittal rapid survey
AcquisitionFree-breathing or single breath-hold; real-time or lightly segmented
ContrastBright blood — excellent blood/myocardium contrast for chamber identification
ECG gatingOptional — often ungated for speed
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquired immediately after HASTE as the second localizer step
  • Transaxial TruFi used to identify LV long-axis and apex to plan the vertical long-axis (2CH) view
  • Bright blood contrast makes cardiac chambers clearly visible — complements HASTE dark blood
  • Multiple planes acquirable in under 60 seconds — use all 3 for best planning geometry
  • In poor breath-holders, real-time ungated TruFi still provides adequate landmark identification
Indications & clinical context

The bright blood bSSFP scout is the second mandatory localizer, acquired immediately after HASTE. Its bright blood contrast makes cardiac chambers clearly visible for accurate planning of the 2-chamber view from the transaxial plane. Together with HASTE, TruFi forms the two-step localizer workflow that begins every CMR study.

  • All CMR protocols (mandatory second localizer)
Dixon Fat Suppression
Tissue Characterisation
Technical parameters
Sequence typeGradient echo — multi-point Dixon (2-point, 3-point or mDixon) chemical shift encoding
WeightingT1-weighted; generates separate water-only, fat-only, in-phase and out-of-phase images
PlanesTargeted to the region of interest (e.g. tumour, mass, pericardium, myocardium); axial or short-axis
Slice thickness5–8 mm (thinner for small lesions)
AcquisitionBreath-hold (BH) preferred; navigator-gated for free-breathing acquisition
Fat suppression qualitySuperior to STIR and spectral fat sat in regions of B0 inhomogeneity (e.g. near implants, lung interfaces)
ECG gatingMid-diastolic trigger; prospective or retrospective
Acquisition recommendations
  • Acquire in-phase (IP), out-of-phase (OP), water-only (W) and fat-only (F) images — all generated from a single acquisition
  • Fat identification: signal drop on OP vs IP image confirms intracellular/microscopic fat (lipid); complete signal loss on water image confirms macroscopic fat
  • Cardiac mass assessment: use Dixon to characterise lipoma, liposarcoma, ARVC fatty infiltration, and intramyocardial fat in infarction or fibro-fatty replacement
  • Pericardial fat: Dixon clearly delineates epicardial and pericardial fat — useful in pericardial vs myocardial disease distinction
  • Ensure B0 shimming is optimised before acquisition — poor shimming degrades Dixon fat-water separation
  • Compare with pre-contrast T1 TSE: fat appears bright on T1 and drops on fat-sat T1 and water-only Dixon
  • If near metallic implants, Dixon outperforms spectral fat suppression — consider as first choice in these patients
Indications & clinical context

Dixon is the preferred fat suppression method for characterising fat-containing cardiac masses and pericardial lesions. It confirms lipomatous content in lipoma, lipomatous hypertrophy of the IAS, and intramyocardial fat. In ACM/ARVC, Dixon can demonstrate fatty or fibro-fatty myocardial replacement, though intramyocardial fat on CMR has low specificity as a standalone criterion. Dixon is also used when spectral fat suppression fails due to B0 inhomogeneity.

  • Cardiac Mass
  • ACM / ARVC
  • Pericardium
Image typeFat appearanceWater appearanceClinical use
In-phase (IP)Bright (fat+water signals add)BrightReference image
Out-of-phase (OP)Signal drop vs IPUnchangedDetect microscopic/intracellular fat
Water-only (W)Dark/absentBrightConfirm macroscopic fat; clean anatomical reference
Fat-only (F)BrightDark/absentMap fat distribution; quantify fat fraction
Study planner

Enter a clinical question or indication below and press Enter. Relevant sequences will be highlighted. Use natural language — e.g. "cardiomyopathy", "severity of AS", "rule out myocarditis", "iron overload", "aortic dissection".

3D MRA
Left ventricular volumes & function — adults

Kawel-Boehm et al, JCMR 2020 · Tables 2–4 · Papillary muscles included in LV mass · bSSFP 1.5T & 3T

LV and RV contouring method from Kawel-Boehm et al. Red contour = endocardium; yellow contour = epicardium (for LV mass); blue contour = outer RV boundary; green contours = papillary muscles (included in LV volume, excluded from LV mass in this dataset). Consistent contouring is essential for serial comparisons.

LV end-diastolic diameter (4CH): men 52±5mm (42–62); women 49±5mm (39–59). Normal LV EF ≥55%; LVM/BSA higher in men at all ages; volumes decrease with age.

RV contouring method. Blue contour = outer RV boundary (epicardium); red contour = RV endocardium. Papillary muscles and moderator band are included within the RV cavity (not excluded). RV mass measured from epicardial minus endocardial volumes × myocardial density.

RVEF in athlete's heart always >50%; RVEF ≤45% in ARVC. RV volumes decrease with age. Men have larger absolute and BSA-indexed volumes than women at all ages.

Left & right atrial volumes — adults

Kawel-Boehm et al, JCMR 2020 · Tables 14–18 · Biplane area-length method, LA appendage excluded · Max volume at ventricular end-systole

LA volumes (biplane area-length, appendage excluded)
ParameterMen mean ± SDMen LL–ULWomen mean ± SDWomen LL–UL
Max LA volume (mL)72 ± 2031–11264 ± 1828–100

LA ejection fraction (%)62 ± 846–7763 ± 848–78
LA biplane area-length measurement method. Left (2CH, yellow): A2C = LA planimetric area; L2C = LA longitudinal diameter; T2C = LA transverse diameter on 2-chamber view. Centre (4CH, red): A4C, L4C, T4C — same measurements on 4-chamber view. Right (3CH, green): A3C = LA area on 3-chamber view; APD = anteroposterior diameter. LA biplane volume = (0.85 × A2C × A4C) / mean(L2C, L4C). Appendage and pulmonary veins excluded.
