Aortic measurement sites (Kawel-Boehm et al, JCMR 2020). AS = sinus of Valsalva (aortic sinuses); STJ = sinotubular junction; AA = tubular ascending aorta; BCA = brachiocephalic artery level (proximal arch); T1/T2 = aortic arch (pre/post-left subclavian); IR = isthmus/proximal descending; DA = mid-descending thoracic aorta; D = diaphragmatic level. Measure perpendicular to vessel centreline in end-diastole.
Thoracic aorta (cross-sectional bSSFP, luminal, diastole)
LevelMen mean ± SD (LL–UL)Women mean ± SD (LL–UL)
Ascending aorta27±4 mm (19–34)26±4 mm (18–33)
Proximal descending aorta21±3 mm (15–26)19±2 mm (15–23)
Distal descending aorta18±3 mm (13–23)16±2 mm (12–20)
Ascending aorta by age (45–84y, MESA, phase contrast)
Age (years)Men median (5th–95th centile)Women median (5th–95th centile)
45–5432 mm (27–37)29 mm (25–34)
55–6433 mm (28–41)30 mm (26–36)
65–7434 mm (29–41)31 mm (26–36)
75–8435 mm (29–41)31 mm (27–37)
Main & branch pulmonary arteries
ParameterMen mean ± SDMen LL–ULWomen mean ± SDWomen LL–UL
MPA systolic diameter (mm)27.4 ± 2.621–3325.3 ± 2.619–31
MPA diastolic diameter (mm)22.9 ± 2.419–2721.2 ± 2.117–25
RPA systolic diameter (mm)20.2 ± 2.914–2617.8 ± 2.414–22
LPA systolic diameter (mm)20.1 ± 2.416–2418.4 ± 2.114–22

Measure in end-diastole on cine bSSFP — MRA overestimates by averaging pulsation. Surgical threshold: root >45mm (men), >40mm (women); lower in Marfan/bicuspid AV. MPA diameter > Ao diameter suggests elevated PA pressure.

T1, T2, T2* & ECV reference values

Kawel-Boehm et al, JCMR 2020 · Tables 61–63 · Messroghli et al SCMR consensus 2017 · Site-specific ranges must be established

Native myocardial T1 (pooled weighted means)
Field strengthVendorSequenceMean (ms)LL–UL (ms)
1.5TSiemensMOLLI972885–1059
1.5TSiemensShMOLLI960903–1017
1.5TPhilipsMOLLI989905–1073
3TSiemensMOLLI11961103–1290
3TSiemensShMOLLI11301021–1240
3TPhilipsMOLLI1097964–1230
T1 mapping contouring method (Kawel-Boehm et al). Left: native T1 map — red contour = endocardium, yellow contour = epicardium, green contour = septal ROI for reference tissue. Right: post-contrast T1 map with same contours. Avoid RV insertion points and papillary muscles. Use mid-septal ROI for T2* measurement to avoid susceptibility from adjacent lung.
T2* septal ROI placement for iron quantification. The blue arc marks the mid-septal ROI — positioned within the interventricular septum to avoid adjacent epicardial fat and susceptibility artefact from lung. The triangular exclusion zone removes the RV blood pool from the measurement. Use the same standardised ROI position for serial comparisons. T2* ≥20ms = normal; 14–20ms = mild iron; 10–14ms = moderate; <10ms = severe.
ECV (pooled weighted means)
Field strengthVendorSequenceMean (%)LL–UL (%)
1.5TSiemensMOLLI2620–32
1.5TSiemensShMOLLI2721–33
1.5TPhilipsMOLLI2317–29
3TSiemensMOLLI2620–32
3TSiemensShMOLLI2520–29
Myocardial T2 (pooled weighted means)
Field strengthVendorTechniqueMean (ms)LL–UL (ms)
1.5TSiemensT2-prep bSSFP5545–65
1.5TSiemensFLASH5242–62
1.5TPhilipsGSE5650–62
3TPhilipsGSE4537–53
T2* grading — myocardial iron overload (1.5T)
T2* (ms)GradeClinical action
>20NormalRoutine annual monitoring
14–20Mild iron loadingReview chelation adequacy
10–14Moderate iron loadingConsider intensifying chelation
<10Severe iron loadingUrgent haematology review — high HF risk
Native T1 direction of change
T1 ↑ (elevated)T1 ↓ (reduced)
Oedema (acute MI, myocarditis)Iron overload
Amyloid (AL and ATTR)Anderson-Fabry disease (septum, early)
Interstitial fibrosis (DCM, HCM, sarcoid)Fat/lipid infiltration
Acute infarctionHaemorrhagic infarction (T1 may be elevated)

3T produces ~28% higher native T1 than 1.5T. ECV relatively unaffected by field strength. Site-specific reference ranges mandatory per SCMR guidelines. ECV requires haematocrit ≤24h of CMR. Normal ECV ~25–27%; >40% = amyloid until proven otherwise.

Paediatric reference values (LV & RV)

van der Ven JPG et al, Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2019 · Kawel-Boehm et al, JCMR 2020 · LMS method, 3 European centres, n=141 children age <1–18y

Reference curves use the LMS method. Boys (blue) have larger ventricles than girls (pink); gender differences more marked after puberty. LVEF slightly higher in boys (67% vs 65%); RVEF similar between genders (>50% normal at all ages).

Key paediatric reference points
ParameterNotes
LVEFBoys ~67%, girls ~65% — both >55% at all ages
RVEFNo significant gender difference; >50% normal at all ages
LV/RV volumesIncrease linearly with BSA; use age- and sex-specific z-scores
LA/RA volumes (indexed)LA: P50 ~37–60 mL/m² (boys), ~34–46 mL/m² (girls) aged 6–20y
Aortic sizeBSA-based: ascending Ao = −1.33 + 18.6×BSA⁰·⁵ mm (z-score method)
Aortic PWVIncreases with age; distensibility decreases with age — both gender-independent in children
Paediatric LV reference curves (van der Ven et al, Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2019). Left column (blue) = boys; right column (pink) = girls. Rows show LV EDV, ESV, SV and mass (g) from age 0–18 years. Lines represent 3rd, 10th, 50th, 90th and 97th percentiles. Values increase linearly with age; boys have larger volumes than girls after puberty.
Paediatric RV reference curves (van der Ven et al, Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2019). Left column (blue) = boys; right column (pink) = girls. Rows show RV EDV, ESV, SV and mass (g) from age 0–18 years. RV volumes are slightly larger than LV at all ages in childhood; gender difference less pronounced than for LV until mid-puberty.
Z-score calculation
ParameterFormula
Z-score[(X/M)^L − 1] / (L × S) where X = measured value, L/M/S = LMS parameters for child's age
Normal rangeZ-score −2 to +2 (3rd–97th percentile)
ReferenceFull LMS tables: van der Ven et al 2019; Sarikouch et al 2011 (atria)
Cardiac sarcoidosis — CMR role & diagnostic criteria

CMR is the preferred imaging modality for suspected cardiac sarcoidosis (CS) due to high sensitivity for myocardial inflammation and fibrosis. It complements FDG-PET and is recommended in the workup of unexplained VT, AV block or LV dysfunction in a patient with known or suspected systemic sarcoidosis.

2014 HRS Expert Consensus — CMR criteria for CS
CriterionFindingNotes
LGE patternNon-ischaemic, multifocal, patchy — basal septum, subepicardial or midwallMost sensitive and specific CMR feature
DistributionBasal septum and lateral wall most common; right ventricular involvement in severe diseaseDoes not follow coronary territory
T2 elevationT2 mapping ↑ in active inflammation (oedema)More prominent in acute/active phase
Native T1 ↑Elevated native T1 in affected segmentsDiffuse T1 elevation may indicate occult disease
ECV ↑Expanded interstitial space in scar/fibrosisECV >30% in LGE+ segments
LV dysfunctionReduced LVEF or regional WMASupportive; correlates with LGE extent
CMR protocol for suspected cardiac sarcoidosis
SequencePurposeKey finding
Cine bSSFP (SA + LA)Volumes, EF, wall motionLVEF ↓, regional WMA, RV involvement
T2 mappingActive myocardial inflammation/oedemaT2 >59ms (1.5T) = oedema
Native T1 mappingDiffuse fibrosis/inflammationT1 ↑ in active and chronic disease
ECV mappingInterstitial expansionECV >30% in involved segments
LGE (WB + BB)Fibrosis/scarringPatchy non-ischaemic midwall/subepicardial
Risk stratification
FeatureRisk implication
LGE presentIndependent predictor of VT and mortality — ICD referral consideration
LGE extent >20% LV massHigh risk; consider ICD regardless of LVEF
LVEF <35%Primary prevention ICD indication (HRS 2014)
RV involvement on LGEAssociated with worse prognosis
Active inflammation (T2↑)Warrants immunosuppression — reassess CMR after 3–6 months
Sustained VT at EPSICD implantation recommended regardless of LVEF
References
Chareonthaitawee P et al. FDG-PET/CT in CS. JACC 2017Slart RHJA et al. FDG-PET/CT(A) imaging in CS. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2018Crouser ED et al. Diagnosis and detection of CS. Chest 2020
Myocarditis — CMR diagnosis

CMR is the recommended non-invasive investigation for suspected clinically suspected myocarditis (CSM) when the diagnosis is uncertain after clinical evaluation and troponin/ECG assessment. LLC 2018 updated the original 2009 criteria to incorporate parametric mapping.

Diagnostic criteria — requires ≥1 from each category
CategoryCriterionThreshold / finding
T2-based oedema (≥1 required)T2 mapping elevatedGlobal or regional T2 >59ms (1.5T) or >45ms (3T) — site-specific values recommended
STIR signal intensity ratioMyocardium:skeletal muscle SI ratio ≥2.0
T1-based myocardial injury (≥1 required)T1 mapping / ECV elevatedNative T1 or ECV above site-specific upper limit of normal
Non-ischaemic LGE≥1 focal lesion, subepicardial or midwall, subendocardium spared
Supportive (increase diagnostic confidence — not required)
Supportive criterionNotes
Pericardial effusionIndicates myopericarditis; more common in viral aetiology
Regional or global LV dysfunction (LVEF ↓)Correlates with troponin elevation and clinical severity
RV involvementMost powerful predictor of death and transplantation
Diagnostic performance
Criteria metSensitivitySpecificityAccuracy
LLC 2018 (≥1 T2 + ≥1 T1 criterion)87%96%91%
Original LLC 200976%96%85%
LGE alone74%86%79%
T2 STIR alone56%92%70%
Follow-up recommendations
ScenarioRecommendation
1 criterion met or strong clinical suspicionRepeat CMR at 1–2 weeks
Confirmed myocarditis — first presentationRepeat CMR at 3–6 months to assess resolution
Persistent LGE at follow-upRisk stratification for arrhythmia (Holter, EPS if indicated)
LVEF <50% at presentationGuideline-directed medical therapy; repeat CMR at 3 months
Athletes with myocarditisReturn to sport only after CMR normalisation (≥3–6 months)
References
Ferreira VM et al. CMR in myocarditis: LLC 2018 update. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2018Ammirati E et al. ESC position statement on myocarditis. Eur Heart J 2022
Cardiac masses — CMR characterisation

CMR is the reference standard for characterising cardiac masses, providing tissue characterisation unavailable with echocardiography. The primary aim is to distinguish tumour from thrombus, define whether a mass is benign or malignant, and guide surgical or oncological management. Remember the three general rules: (1) thrombus is the most frequent intracavitary "tumour"; (2) secondary neoplasms are more frequent than primary; (3) benign primary tumours are 3× more common than malignant.

Systematic CMR approach to a cardiac mass

Assess each mass in four steps: size & localisation (including patient age and sex) → morphology → signal intensity characteristics → dynamic and equilibrium enhancement.

StepWhat to assessClinical relevance
1. Size & locationChamber of origin, attachment point, extracardiac extension, pericardial/pleural involvementLocation guides differential (e.g. RA → angiosarcoma; LA fossa ovalis → myxoma; valve → fibroelastoma)
2. MorphologyShape, borders, attachment (pedunculated vs sessile vs intramural), multiplicity, invasionMalignant morphology: large (>5 cm), irregular borders, direct invasion, pericardial nodules, multiple lesions
3. Signal intensityT1w and T2w characteristics; fat saturation; STIR; T2* if haemorrhage suspectedIdentifies fat (lipoma), haemorrhage (angiosarcoma, thrombus), water content (myxoma, cyst), fibrous tissue (fibroma)
4. EnhancementFirst-pass perfusion; early gadolinium enhancement (EGE); late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) with standard and long TINo enhancement at any TI = thrombus. Heterogeneous LGE = myxoma/sarcoma. Dense homogeneous = fibroma. Intense early = haemangioma
CMR protocol for cardiac mass
Mandatory sequencesOptional sequences
Black-blood FSE T1w (multiple planes)First-pass perfusion through mass
Black-blood FSE T2w + fat saturation (or STIR)Phase-contrast velocity mapping (haemodynamic impact)
Cine bSSFP (standard planes + mass-specific)Tagging (to distinguish mass from adjacent myocardium)
Post-gadolinium T1w: early IR-GRE (EGE), black-blood FSE, late IR-GRE (LGE)T1 and T2 mapping (emerging)
LGE TIP: Use both standard TI (200–300 ms, nulling myocardium) AND long TI (>600 ms). Thrombus remains dark ("always black") at both TIs, distinguishing it from tumour which nulls at one but not the other.
Relative incidence of primary cardiac tumours
Benign (~70–75%)Malignant (~25–30%)
Tumour%Tumour%
Myxoma30%Angiosarcoma9%
Lipoma10%Rhabdomyosarcoma6%
Papillary fibroelastoma10%Mesothelioma4%
Rhabdomyoma (paediatric)8%Fibrosarcoma3%
Fibroma4%Lymphoma2%
Teratoma3%Other sarcomas3%
Other benign5%Other malignant<2%

Note: Cardiac metastases are ×40 more common than primary tumours. High-propensity primaries: melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, breast, lung, haematological malignancies.

Location-based differential diagnosis
LocationConsider
Left atrium (fossa ovalis)Myxoma (75% of myxomas), thrombus (appendage/wall), fibrosarcoma, osteosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma (posterior wall)
Right atriumAngiosarcoma ★, lymphoma, thrombus, myxoma (20%), leiomyomatosis (IVC extension)
VentriclesFibroma (intramural, LV/IVS), rhabdomyoma (paediatric, intramural), thrombus (akinetic segments/aneurysm)
ValvularPapillary fibroelastoma ★ (90% valvular), infective vegetations
PericardiumPericardial cyst (low T1, high T2, no enhancement), mesothelioma, metastases
Any chamberLipoma (intramural or intracavitary), haemangioma (intracavitary), rhabdomyosarcoma (intramural), metastases
Malignant morphological features — red flags
Morphological featureTissue characteristics of malignancy
Large size (usually >5 cm)Signal heterogeneity on T1 and T2 (haemorrhage and necrosis)
Irregular, ill-defined bordersHaemorrhagic pericardial effusion (high T1 SI)
Direct myocardial invasionHeterogeneous contrast enhancement
Right heart or IVC involvementSpecial case: high T1 + low T2 → metastatic melanoma
Pericardial/pleural involvement (effusions, nodules)Rapid growth on serial imaging
Multiple lesionsInvolvement of >1 cardiac chamber
CMR tissue characterisation — full signal characteristics table
MassT1wT2wLGE / EGEKey distinguishing feature
PSEUDOTUMOURS
Thrombus (acute)HighHighNo uptake at any TIAlways dark on LGE regardless of TI — pathognomonic
Thrombus (subacute)HighLowNo uptakeLocation: LA appendage, akinetic/aneurysmal LV
Thrombus (chronic)LowLowNo uptake**Organised thrombus may show peripheral fibrous enhancement
Pericardial cystLowHigh (water)No uptakeSmooth, well-defined; low T1 distinguishes from lipoma
BENIGN TUMOURS
MyxomaIso (low in myxomatous component)High ↑ (heterogeneous)Inhomogeneous enhancementPedunculated from fossa ovalis; very dark on cine (bSSFP) due to low cell density
LipomaHigh ↑ (suppressed on fat sat)High ↑ (suppressed on fat sat)No uptakeFat saturation confirms lipomatous content; no enhancement
Papillary fibroelastomaIsoIsoMinimal/rimSmall (<1 cm), valvular surface, mobile on cine; difficult to see on bbFSE — use cine
FibromaIsoLow ↓ (homogeneous)Intense homogeneous (hyperenhancement)Intramural LV/IVS; low T2 (fibrous, no necrosis); calcifications; children/young adults
RhabdomyomaIsoIso/high ↑No/minimal uptakeMultifocal intramural; paediatric; associated with tuberous sclerosis; DD fibroma: no calcifications, T2 high
HaemangiomaIntermediateVery high ↑↑Homogeneous or inhomogeneous; early or late depending on subtypeIntramural or endocardial; hypervascular; DD angiosarcoma (important)
ParagangliomaIsoHigh ↑Avid enhancementAtrial wall; catecholaminergic (↑ urinary catecholamines); MIBG scintigraphy confirmatory; DD myxoma/angioma
MALIGNANT TUMOURS
AngiosarcomaHeterogeneous (haemorrhage)Heterogeneous ↑Heterogeneous ("sunrise appearance")RA ★; large base; pericardial invasion; haemorrhagic effusion; most common primary malignant cardiac tumour
RhabdomyosarcomaIsoHyperintense ↑HomogeneousAny chamber + valves; most frequent cardiac sarcoma in childhood; pericardial nodules
Undifferentiated sarcomaIsoHyperintense ↑Heterogeneous/variableOften LA (DD myxoma); wide base, invasion; poor prognosis
Lymphoma (primary)Iso/hypoIso/hyperintenseInhomogeneous; minimal in someImmunocompromised hosts; B-cell NHL; ≥2 chambers in 75%; MRI useful for treatment response
MetastasisLow (variable)High (variable)HeterogeneousMost common cardiac malignancy overall; melanoma: high T1 + low T2 (paramagnetic melanin)
Thrombus — age-dependent signal characteristics
Age of thrombusT1w SIT2w SIEGELGE
AcuteHighHighNo uptakeNo uptake
SubacuteHighLowNo uptakeNo uptake
ChronicLowLowNo uptakeNo uptake*
*Organised chronic thrombus may show peripheral enhancement on LGE due to fibrous content. Use long TI (>600 ms) in addition to standard TI to confirm — true thrombus remains black at both.
Benign tumour profiles — key clinical pearls
TumourEpidemiology & associationsSymptomsCMR pearls
MyxomaMost common primary cardiac tumour (30%); F>M; sporadic or Carney complexObstruction (mitral stenosis-like), embolic events, constitutional (fever, weight loss, fatigue)T1 iso/low, T2 high, heterogeneous LGE; pedunculated from fossa ovalis; very dark on cine; DD thrombus (no enhancement)
Fibroelastoma2nd most common (10%); adults; 90% on valve surfaceUsually asymptomatic (autopsy finding); embolic stroke<1 cm; mobile on cine (best sequence for detection); homogeneous; minimal enhancement
FibromaCongenital; 1/3 in children <1 yr; associated with Gorlin syndrome (14%)1/3 asymptomatic; heart failure; arrhythmias; sudden deathIntramural LV/IVS; 2–10 cm; very low T2 (no necrosis, no haemorrhage); calcifications; dense homogeneous LGE — characteristic
RhabdomyomaMost common cardiac tumour in infants; associated with tuberous sclerosis (50%)Obstruction, arrhythmias, may regress spontaneouslyMultifocal intramural; T2 iso/high; no calcifications (DD fibroma); minimal LGE
Haemangioma5–10% of benign tumours; any age; cavernous/capillary/AV subtypesArrhythmias, chest pain; can mimic angiosarcomaVery high T2; variable early/late enhancement by subtype; hypervascular on perfusion
Malignant tumour profiles — key clinical pearls
TumourKey featuresCMR pearls
AngiosarcomaMost common primary malignant cardiac tumour; RA ★; large infiltrating mass with broad base; grows into atrial cavity AND pericardium → tamponade; haemorrhagic pericardial effusionHeterogeneous T1 (haemorrhage); heterogeneous T2 ↑; heterogeneous LGE "sunrise appearance" — nodular enhancement within RA mass is characteristic
RhabdomyosarcomaMost frequent cardiac sarcoma in childhood; any chamber; valve involvement common; pericardial involvement → multiple nodulesIso T1; hyperintense T2; homogeneous enhancement; RA>LA>RV>LV
Lymphoma (primary)Rare; immunocompromised hosts (HIV, post-transplant); B-cell NHL; exclusive cardiac/pericardial localisation at diagnosis; ≥2 chambers in 75%Iso/hypo T1, iso T2 (not hyper like other malignancies); inhomogeneous enhancement; MRI used for treatment response monitoring
Metastases×40 more common than primary tumours; highest propensity: melanoma, renal cell, breast, lung, haematological; routes: direct invasion, haematogenous, lymphatic, transvenous (renal/hepatic)Variable SI; melanoma: high T1 + low T2 (paramagnetic melanin — pathognomonic); heterogeneous LGE; pericardial involvement common
Role of hybrid imaging (PET-MRI / PET-CT)

PET-MRI combines functional metabolic data with superior soft-tissue characterisation. SUVmax ≥5.2 combined with MRI morphology achieves 100% sensitivity and 100% specificity for malignancy. MRI alone achieves 100% sensitivity / 92% specificity. T2w hyperintensity and contrast enhancement alone each achieve 100% sensitivity but low specificity (~46–54%). PET is particularly useful when MRI findings are equivocal and for staging/treatment response in confirmed malignancy.

PET-MRI featureSensitivitySpecificityPPVNPV
SUVmax ≥5.2 (optimal cutoff)100%92%88%100%
MRI overall assessment100%92%88%100%
MRI + SUVmax ≥5.2 (combined)100%100%100%100%
T2w hyperintensity alone100%54%54%100%
Contrast enhancement alone100%46%50%100%
Cine SSFP morphology86%92%86%92%
Pseudotumours — normal variants & non-neoplastic masses

Several normal structures or non-neoplastic lesions can mimic cardiac masses. Always consider these before diagnosing tumour:

PseudotumourLocationCMR features
Crista terminalisRA lateral wallNormal muscular ridge; follows myocardium on all sequences; no enhancement beyond myocardium
Pericardial cystRight cardiophrenic angle (most common)Low T1, high T2 (water), no enhancement; smooth; asymptomatic
Lipomatous hypertrophy of IASInteratrial septum (spares fossa ovalis → "dumbbell" shape)High T1 suppressed with fat sat; no enhancement; can cause arrhythmia
Mitral annulus calcification (MAC)Posterior mitral annulusLow SI all sequences; no enhancement; CT confirms calcification
Hiatus herniaPosterior to LAExtrinsic compression; follows stomach/bowel signal; no myocardial invasion
Hydatid cystAny myocardial locationLow T1, high T2; daughter cysts; no enhancement; serology for Echinococcus
References
Motwani M et al. MR of cardiac tumours and masses. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2019 Grani C et al. Multimodality imaging in cardiac masses. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2020 Rahbar K et al. PET-MRI for cardiac masses. J Nucl Med 2015;56:255–260 Roberts WC. Primary and secondary neoplasms of the heart. Am J Cardiol 1997;80:671–82 SCMR position paper on clinical indications for CMR. JCMR 2020
Cardiac amyloidosis — CMR diagnosis & subtyping

CMR provides structural, functional and tissue characterisation data essential for diagnosing and subtyping cardiac amyloidosis. Differentiation of AL from ATTR subtypes is critical as treatments differ fundamentally.

CMR features of cardiac amyloidosis
FeatureFindingNotes
LGE patternGlobal subendocardial ± transmural; inability to null myocardiumHallmark sign — inability to null despite correct TI
LGE distributionCircumferential subendocardial extending to transmural in ATTRSubendocardial LGE more typical of AL
Native T1Markedly elevated throughout LV myocardiumMost elevated in ATTR; T1 >1050ms (1.5T) highly suggestive
ECV>40% in both subtypes; higher in ATTRECV correlates with amyloid burden and prognosis
LV morphologyConcentric thickening, non-dilated, biventricular involvementGranular sparkling on echo; MRI more specific
AtriaDilated, thickened IAS (>6mm), LA appendage thrombus riskIAS thickening >6mm highly specific for amyloid
ValvesThickened atrioventricular valvesAmyloid infiltration
Pericardial effusionSmall effusion commonSupportive finding
AL vs ATTR differentiation by CMR
ParameterAL amyloidATTR amyloid
QALE (T1 blood ÷ T1 myocardium)<13 (inverted ratio)≥13 (preserved ratio)
LV mass/BSA<100 g/m²>100 g/m² (usually >120 g/m²)
LGE patternPredominantly subendocardialTransmural / diffuse
ECVElevated (>40%)Very elevated (>45–50%); higher than AL
Native T1ElevatedHigher than AL; often >1200ms (1.5T)
LVEFMay be preserved early; worse prognosisUsually preserved until late disease
PrognosisWorse despite less fibrosisBetter with TTR-targeted therapy
TreatmentChemotherapy (anti-plasma cell)Tafamidis / diflunisal / patisiran (TTR-targeted)
CMR in the multimodality algorithm
InvestigationRole
CMR (LGE + T1/ECV)Tissue characterisation, subtyping, prognostication, treatment monitoring
Technetium pyrophosphate (99mTc-PYP) scintigraphyHigh sensitivity/specificity for ATTR; Grade 2–3 = ATTR without biopsy
Serum/urine free light chains + SPEPExclude AL before accepting ATTR diagnosis non-invasively
Genetic testing (TTR mutation)Distinguish hereditary (variant) from wild-type ATTR
Endomyocardial biopsyGold standard — required if non-invasive criteria not met
References
Fontana M et al. Prognostic value of late gadolinium in cardiac amyloid. Circulation 2015Kotecha T et al. Myocardial ECV quantification in amyloidosis. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2018Dorbala S et al. Amyloidosis multimodality imaging. JACC 2021
CMR with cardiac implantable electronic devices (CIEDs)

The majority of modern CIEDs can be scanned safely at 1.5T under a conditional protocol. 3T scanning remains contraindicated for most devices. Pre-scan programming and post-scan device check are mandatory.

MR-conditional device requirements (1.5T)
RequirementStandard
Field strength1.5T only (3T contraindicated for most CIEDs)
Device labellingMR-conditional labelling for both generator and all leads
Lead ageTypically ≥6 weeks post-implant (check device-specific IFU)
SAR limit≤2.0 W/kg whole body — most conditional devices allow normal CMR protocols
Gradient dB/dtPer device IFU — standard cardiac sequences usually comply
Post-scan checkMandatory — sensing, pacing threshold, lead impedance
Pre-scan device programming (pacemaker-dependent patients)
Patient typeRecommended programmingRationale
Pacemaker-dependentAsynchronous pacing (DOO/VOO) at rate 10–20 bpm above intrinsicPrevents inhibition by RF noise
Non-pacemaker-dependentOOO (sensing/pacing off) or asynchronous modeEliminates inappropriate tracking/inhibition
ICD / CRT-DTachyarrhythmia therapies OFF; pacing as abovePrevents inappropriate shock from RF artefact
All patientsContinuous monitoring; resuscitation equipment immediately availableSafety standard
Common MR-conditional devices (examples — always verify current IFU)
Device typeExamples (MR-conditional)Key conditions
PacemakerMedtronic Advisa MRI, Abbott Assurity MRI, Boston Scientific INGENIO MRI1.5T; conditional leads only; IFU programming required
ICDMedtronic Evera MRI, Abbott Ellipse MRI, Boston Scientific RESONATE MRI1.5T; tachytherapies off; conditional leads
CRT-P/DMedtronic Percepta MRI, Abbott Quadra Assura MRI1.5T; all leads must be conditional; LV lead age ≥6 weeks
Subcutaneous ICD (S-ICD)Boston Scientific Emblem MRI1.5T conditional; no pacing lead; MRI artefact over sternum
Leadless pacemakerMedtronic Micra AV, Abbott Aveir1.5T conditional; no lead concerns
Absolute contraindications to CMR
Device/situationReason
Non-conditional CIED (legacy device)Risk of lead heating, programming changes, device malfunction — only in exceptional clinical need with specialist centre oversight
Epicardial leads (abandoned/active)High heating risk — insufficient data for routine scanning
3T with most CIEDsExceeds SAR/gradient limits of most device IFUs
Temporary pacing wireAbsolute contraindication — uninsulated leads
Cochlear implant (most)Ferromagnetic components — check device specific
References
Indik JH et al. HRS expert consensus on MRI and CIED. Heart Rhythm 2017Russo RJ et al. Assessing the risks of MRI in CIED patients. NEJM 2017
MRI safety — contraindications & gadolinium
Absolute contraindications to MRI
ItemReasonAction
Non-MR-conditional cochlear implantFerromagnetic components; magnet displacementDo not scan — check device IFU
Ferromagnetic intracranial aneurysm clipTorque force causing clip displacementDo not scan unless clip confirmed MR-safe
Intraorbital metallic foreign bodyTorque causing ocular injuryX-ray orbits to exclude; do not scan if present
Temporary pacing wireUninsulated leads; heating and current inductionAbsolute contraindication
Some neurostimulators / DBSHeating of leads; stimulationCheck device-specific IFU; may be conditional
Relative contraindications / conditional items
ItemStatusNotes
Non-conditional CIED (legacy pacemaker/ICD)Relative — high riskOnly with specialist centre oversight, risk-benefit discussion, monitoring team on standby
MR-conditional CIEDConditional — safe with protocol1.5T only; all leads must be conditional; IFU programming; post-scan check mandatory
Prosthetic heart valvesConditional — most MR-safe at 1.5T and 3TConfirm prosthesis type and IFU — the vast majority are safe
Sternal wires / mediastinal clipsSafe — no restrictionPost-surgical clips are MR-safe; no clinical restriction
Coronary stentsSafe after 6 weeksMost modern stents are MR-conditional or MR-safe; check if uncertain
Pregnancy (1st trimester)Relative — avoid if possibleNo ionising radiation; avoid gadolinium in 1st trimester; use if clinical benefit outweighs risk
ClaustrophobiaRelativeSedation protocol; inform patient in advance; open-bore scanner if available
Gadolinium contrast agents — safety
ParameterStandard doseNotes
Standard dose0.1 mmol/kg (0.2 mL/kg for 0.5 M agents)Adequate for LGE, perfusion and MRA in most protocols
Double dose0.2 mmol/kgSometimes used for perfusion studies — less common with modern sequences
Injection rate (LGE)2–3 mL/s + 20 mL saline flushSlower rates acceptable for LGE if peripheral access is poor
Injection rate (perfusion)3–7 mL/s + 30 mL saline flushHigh rate essential for first-pass bolus integrity
Paediatric dosing0.1–0.2 mmol/kg (weight-based)Use macrocyclic agent; weight-based calculation essential
Gadolinium agent classes
ClassExamplesNSF riskRecommended use
Macrocyclic (preferred)Gadoterate (Dotarem), Gadobutrol (Gadovist), Gadoteridol (ProHance)Lowest — Group IFirst choice; use in all patients including reduced eGFR
Linear ionicGadopentetate (Magnevist), Gadoxetate (Primovist)Intermediate — Group IIAvoid if eGFR <30; hepatobiliary specific agents have distinct pharmacokinetics
Linear non-ionicGadodiamide (Omniscan), Gadoversetamide (OptiMARK)Highest — Group IIIContraindicated if eGFR <30; avoid where possible — NSF risk highest
Nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF) & eGFR thresholds
eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)Risk categoryRecommendation
>30Low riskStandard macrocyclic agent; no restriction
15–30Increased riskMacrocyclic agent only; minimum effective dose; document clinical necessity
<15 or dialysisHigh riskMacrocyclic agent only; avoid linear agents; risk-benefit discussion; consider non-contrast CMR
AKI (any eGFR)High riskDefer until renal function has stabilised; AKI dramatically increases NSF risk
PregnancySpecial considerationAvoid gadolinium in 1st trimester; macrocyclic only if essential; breastfeeding — no interruption required with macrocyclic agents (ESUR 2023)
References
ESUR Contrast Media Safety Guidelines v11 2023 MRIsafety.com — Shellock RF ACR Manual on Contrast Media 2023
Stress agents — adenosine & regadenoson
Adenosine — protocol
ParameterStandardNotes
Dose140 mcg/kg/min IV infusionIncrease to 170–210 mcg/kg/min if inadequate response (no symptoms, no HR rise)
Infusion duration≥3 minutes before contrast injection; continue for ≥1 min afterMinimum 3 min to achieve steady-state hyperaemia
RoutePeripheral IV (antecubital preferred); separate line for contrastCan use same arm if two lines available; avoid same cannula
MonitoringContinuous ECG, SpO2, BP every 1 min, symptomsPhysician or advanced nurse practitioner must be present throughout
Caffeine washout≥24 hours before scanCaffeine competitively antagonises adenosine A2A receptors — blunts hyperaemia
Dipyridamole washout≥48 hours before scanDipyridamole potentiates adenosine — risk of profound bradycardia/bronchospasm
Half-life<10 secondsEffects resolve rapidly on stopping infusion — theophylline reversal rarely needed
Adenosine — contraindications
ContraindicationCategoryRationale
2nd or 3rd degree AV block (without pacemaker)AbsoluteAdenosine causes AV nodal slowing — risk of complete block
Sick sinus syndrome (without pacemaker)AbsoluteRisk of sinus arrest
Severe reactive airways disease / active wheezeAbsoluteA1 receptor-mediated bronchoconstriction — risk of severe bronchospasm
Known hypersensitivity to adenosineAbsolute—
Dipyridamole use within 48hAbsolutePotentiation — profound haemodynamic effects
Systolic BP <90 mmHgAbsoluteAdenosine causes peripheral vasodilation — risk of hypotension
Mild/moderate asthma (well-controlled, not wheezing)RelativeConsider regadenoson or dobutamine as alternative; proceed with caution
Severe aortic stenosisRelativeFixed output state; vasodilation may cause severe hypotension
Recent STEMI (<2 days)RelativeHaemodynamic instability risk
Adenosine — side effects & management
Side effectFrequencyManagement
Chest tightness / flushing / dyspnoeaVery common (~80%)Reassure — expected; resolve within 30–60s of stopping infusion
HeadacheCommonReassure; resolves on stopping
AV block (1st degree)CommonMonitor; usually transient; stop if 2nd/3rd degree develops
2nd/3rd degree AV blockUncommonStop infusion immediately; usually self-limiting (<10s); IV theophylline 100mg if prolonged
BronchospasmRareStop immediately; salbutamol inhaler; IV aminophylline if severe
Severe hypotensionRareStop infusion; IV fluids; leg raise; IV atropine if bradycardic
Cardiac arrestVery rareFull resuscitation; resus equipment must be immediately available
Regadenoson — protocol & comparison
ParameterRegadenosonvs Adenosine
Dose0.4 mg (5 mL) single rapid IV bolus over 10 secondsBolus vs continuous infusion — simpler administration
MechanismSelective A2A receptor agonistMore A2A-selective than adenosine — fewer A1/A2B/A3 side effects
Onset of peak hyperaemia~30–60 seconds post-injectionSimilar onset to adenosine
Duration of hyperaemia2–4 minutes (longer than adenosine)Allows more imaging time; also means side effects last longer
Half-life~2–3 minutes (initial); elimination ~30 minLonger than adenosine — aminophylline reversal may be needed
Caffeine washout≥12 hours (some centres use 24h)Same A2A receptor competition — caffeine still reduces hyperaemia
Splenic switch-offNot reliableCannot use splenic sign to confirm adequate vasodilation
AV block riskLower than adenosineA2A selectivity spares A1 nodal effects — less AV block
Bronchospasm riskLower than adenosineCan be used cautiously in mild/moderate asthma — check local policy
Reversal agentAminophylline 75–100 mg IV slow bolusMay be needed given longer half-life vs adenosine
Resuscitation equipment — mandatory for all stress CMR
ItemRequirement
Crash trolleyImmediately outside scanner room or in adjacent area
MR-conditional defibrillatorAvailable in/adjacent to scanner room
Aminophylline 250 mg/10 mLDrawn up and immediately available — adenosine/regadenoson reversal
Salbutamol inhalerAvailable — bronchospasm management
IV atropine 0.6 mgAvailable — bradycardia management
Clinician presentPhysician or advanced nurse practitioner throughout stress infusion
References
Nagel E et al. CMR stress testing recommendations. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2017 Antiochos P et al. Regadenoson vs adenosine CMR perfusion. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2021
CMR artefact recognition
Cine bSSFP artefacts
ArtefactAppearanceCauseSolution
Banding / dark band artefactDark stripes across image at regular intervalsOff-resonance (B0 inhomogeneity) — bSSFP is highly sensitive to off-resonanceRe-shim; adjust centre frequency; use GRE cine instead; reduce TR
Bright blood ghostingBlurred copies of bright blood structures offset in phase directionPulsatile flow in phase-encode direction; incomplete breath-holdChange phase-encode direction; use cardiac triggering; improve breath-hold
Motion blurringBlurred myocardial borders; poor temporal resolutionBreathing during acquisition; arrhythmiaRepeat breath-hold; reduce phases; use real-time cine
Arrhythmia / triggering failureGhosting; "venetian blind" artifact; frame dropoutEctopics or AF causing inconsistent R-R intervalsReal-time cine; arrhythmia rejection; prospective triggering
LGE artefacts
ArtefactAppearanceCauseSolution / implications
Incorrect TI (over/under-nulling)Myocardium appears bright (TI too short) or signal heterogeneous (TI too long)TI not optimised for current T1 of myocardiumRepeat TI scout; use PSIR which is TI-robust
Subendocardial dark rim artefactDark band at subendocardium on LGE and perfusionGibbs truncation / ringing artefact; partial volumeIncrease matrix; use black blood LGE; present in ≥3 phases = true LGE
Motion ghostingCopies of bright LGE signal offset in phase directionBreathing; arrhythmia during acquisitionMotion-corrected (MOCO) reconstruction; repeat with better breath-hold
Wraparound (aliasing)Anatomy from outside FOV folds into imageFOV too small for patient anatomyIncrease FOV; apply saturation band outside FOV; oversampling in phase direction
Metallic susceptibilitySignal void / distortion near implantLocal B0 distortion from metallic device (sternal wires, valves)Use WARP/slice-encoding for metal artefact correction (SEMAC/MAVRIC); black blood LGE less affected
Perfusion artefacts
ArtefactAppearanceCauseSolution
Dark band / Gibbs artefactDark rim affecting entire subendocardium globally, all territories simultaneouslyTruncation artefact from high-contrast bolus arrivalDistinguish from true defect: true defect is focal, territory-specific, persists >3 frames
Cardiac motion artefactBlurring or misregistration of perfusion slicesBreath-hold failure; arrhythmiaFree-breathing perfusion; motion-corrected reconstruction
Parallel imaging artefactFold-over in centre of image when acceleration factor too highg-factor noise amplification from GRAPPA/SENSEReduce acceleration factor; adjust PE direction
Mapping artefacts
ArtefactSequenceAppearanceCause & solution
Breathing motionT1 / T2 mappingMisregistration between inversion images; incorrect T1/T2 fitBreath-hold coaching; motion-corrected MOLLI variants
Heart rate variabilityMOLLI T1T1 overestimation in tachycardia; T1 underestimation with ectopicsUse SASHA (heart rate independent) or ShMOLLI; arrhythmia rejection
T2* susceptibility near RV insertionT2*Artificially low T2* at RV insertion points due to local B0 distortionAvoid placing ROI near RV insertion; use septal mid-wall ROI only
Fat-water swapping (Dixon)DixonIncorrect fat/water assignment — water appears dark, fat appears bright (swapped)Indicates poor B0 shimming; re-shim and repeat; use 3-point Dixon
Key rule: dark rim artefact vs true subendocardial LGE
FeatureDark rim artefact (Gibbs)True subendocardial LGE
LocationEntire circumference of subendocardium globallyRegional — follows coronary territory
TimingWorst at peak bolus; resolves on later framesPersists on all frames including delayed imaging
Phase-encode directionShifts with phase-encode direction changeDoes not change with phase-encode direction
Thickness1–2 pixels thinUsually >2 pixels; visible on multiple slice positions
Black blood LGEUsually absentConfirmed on black blood LGE
References
Biglands JD et al. Cardiovascular MRI artefacts. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2015 Kramer CM et al. SCMR/EACVI standardised CMR protocols 2020
Extracardiac findings on CMR

Extracardiac findings are identified in up to 25–30% of CMR studies. Clinically significant incidental findings requiring follow-up occur in approximately 5–7% of scans. All CMR reports should include a systematic review of visible extracardiac structures.

Systematic review checklist
StructureWhat to assessCommon findings
LungsParenchymal abnormalities, consolidation, nodules, massesPleural effusions, atelectasis, lung nodules, fibrosis, consolidation
PleuraEffusions, thickeningPleural effusions (often bilateral in heart failure), pleural plaques
MediastinumLymphadenopathy, massesMediastinal lymphadenopathy (sarcoidosis, lymphoma, metastatic disease), thymic mass
Great vesselsAortic aneurysm, dissection, coarctation, pulmonary embolismAortic dilatation, atheroma, right aortic arch, aberrant subclavian
LiverHepatomegaly, focal lesions, iron loading (T2*)Hepatic cysts, haemangiomas, hepatic congestion, iron overload
SpleenSplenomegaly, focal lesionsSplenomegaly (haematological conditions), accessory spleen
KidneysSize, cysts, masses, hydronephrosisSimple renal cysts (very common), solid renal mass (needs follow-up), asymmetry
Spine & chest wallVertebral body signal, fractures, bone lesionsVertebral haemangiomas, compression fractures, degenerative change
Upper abdomenAdrenal glands, gallbladder, pancreas (if visible)Adrenal adenoma, gallstones, abdominal lymphadenopathy
BreastIncidental lesions (on axial images)Enhancing breast mass — refer for dedicated breast imaging
Clinical significance grading
GradeDefinitionActionExamples
Not significantNormal variant or clearly benign findingNo follow-up required; document in reportSimple renal cyst, vertebral haemangioma, small pericardial effusion
IndeterminateFinding that may be clinically relevantRecommend follow-up imaging or clinical correlationLung nodule >6mm, adrenal mass >1cm, mediastinal lymphadenopathy
SignificantFinding requiring urgent or prompt actionCommunicate directly to referrer; document clearlyPulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, large pleural effusion, suspicious mass
Lung nodule follow-up (Fleischner Society 2017)
Nodule sizeLow riskHigh risk
<6mmNo routine follow-upOptional CT at 12 months
6–8mmCT at 6–12 monthsCT at 6–12 months, then consider 18–24 months
>8mmCT at 3 months, PET/CT, or tissue sampling

Note: CMR is not optimised for lung parenchymal assessment. Low-dose CT should be recommended for any indeterminate pulmonary finding.

Key sequences for extracardiac review
SequenceBest forTip
HASTE (axial)Lung parenchyma, pleural effusions, mediastinal masses, liver/spleenReview all slices systematically — this is the primary extracardiac survey sequence
Cine bSSFPGreat vessel anatomy, pericardial effusion, chest wallScroll through SA stack beyond the heart — look for vertebral and chest wall abnormalities
LGE (PSIR)Incidental enhancing lesions (liver, kidney, bone)Enhancing renal or hepatic lesions may be visible on LGE sequences
3D MRA (if acquired)Aortic anatomy, pulmonary arteries, renal arteriesReconstruct MIP images for PE and renal artery stenosis
  • Always review HASTE localizer systematically — most extracardiac findings are identified here
  • Report all findings — even clearly benign ones — to demonstrate systematic review
  • Significant or unexpected findings should be communicated directly to the referrer
  • Recommend dedicated imaging (CT, ultrasound) for indeterminate findings — CMR is not optimised for extracardiac assessment
References
McKenna DA et al. Extracardiac findings on cardiac MRI. AJR Am J Roentgenol 2008 MacMahon H et al. Fleischner Society guidelines for pulmonary nodules. Radiology 2017 Defined reporting of extracardiac findings on CMR. JCMR 2020
Ischaemia & viability — CMR assessment

CMR provides a comprehensive ischaemia and viability assessment in a single examination, combining stress perfusion, rest perfusion, LGE, and functional analysis. This allows simultaneous detection of inducible ischaemia, quantification of scar burden, assessment of myocardial viability, and evaluation of ventricular function — information that directly guides revascularisation decisions.

Stress perfusion interpretation
FindingStress perfusionRest perfusionLGEInterpretation
Inducible ischaemiaPerfusion defectNormalNo LGEViable ischaemic myocardium — revascularisation likely to benefit
Peri-infarct ischaemiaPerfusion defectPartial defectSubendocardial LGEMixed scar and ischaemia — revascularisation may improve symptoms
Fixed defect (scar)Perfusion defectPerfusion defectMatched LGENon-viable scar — revascularisation unlikely to benefit
NormalNormalNormalNo LGENo ischaemia or scar
Dark rim artefactSubendocardial defect — all territories, 1–2 framesMay be presentNo LGEGibbs artefact — not true ischaemia. Global, thin, transient.
LGE transmurality and viability

The transmural extent of LGE is the strongest predictor of functional recovery after revascularisation. Segments with less scar have more viable myocardium and are more likely to recover contractile function.

LGE transmuralityLikelihood of recoveryClinical implication
0% (no LGE)Very high (>80%)Hibernating myocardium if dysfunctional — strong revascularisation benefit
1–25%High (~70%)Predominantly viable — revascularisation recommended
26–50%Intermediate (~50%)Mixed viability — clinical judgement; consider symptoms and territory
51–75%Low (~20%)Predominantly non-viable — revascularisation benefit unlikely
>75%Very low (<5%)Transmural scar — no recovery expected; revascularisation not indicated for viability
Myocardial salvage and acute MI assessment
ParameterSequenceClinical significance
Area at riskT2 STIR or T2 mapping (pre-contrast)Represents the total jeopardised territory — must be acquired before gadolinium
Infarct sizeLGE (≥10 min post-contrast)Represents irreversible necrosis within the area at risk
Myocardial salvage indexMSI = (area at risk − infarct size) / area at riskQuantifies salvaged myocardium after reperfusion — higher = better outcome
Microvascular obstruction (MVO)EGE (1–3 min) or LGE (dark core within bright scar)No-reflow zone — strongest adverse prognostic marker in acute MI
Intramyocardial haemorrhageT2* mapping (dark core within infarct)Indicates reperfusion injury — associated with adverse remodelling
LV thrombusEGE (1–3 min post-contrast)Non-enhancing mass in akinetic/aneurysmal territory — anticoagulation indicated
Hibernation vs stunning
FeatureHibernationStunning
MechanismChronic hypoperfusion — downregulated but viable myocardiumPost-ischaemic dysfunction despite restored perfusion
Wall motionReduced at restReduced at rest (transient)
PerfusionReduced at restNormal at rest
LGENone or minimal (<25% transmurality)None or minimal
Recovery after revascularisationYes — requires revascularisation to recoverYes — spontaneous recovery over days to weeks
CMR clueWMA + reduced rest perfusion + no/minimal LGEWMA + normal rest perfusion + no LGE (recent event)
Ischaemic vs non-ischaemic LGE patterns
FeatureIschaemicNon-ischaemic
DistributionFollows coronary territoryDoes not follow coronary territory
Subendocardial involvementAlways starts subendocardially → extends transmurallySubendocardium typically spared (midwall or subepicardial)
Typical conditionsMI, chronic ischaemic cardiomyopathyMyocarditis, DCM, HCM, sarcoidosis, amyloid
Coronary correlationMatches culprit vessel territoryNo coronary correlation
  • Always acquire STIR/T2 mapping BEFORE contrast — gadolinium suppresses the T2 oedema signal
  • EGE at 1–3 min post-contrast is the best sequence for MVO and LV thrombus detection
  • LGE transmurality ≤25% = likely functional recovery; >75% = unlikely recovery
  • Stress perfusion defect without LGE = viable ischaemic territory — strongest indication for revascularisation
  • MVO (no-reflow) is the single strongest adverse prognostic marker in acute STEMI
References
Nagel E et al. MR-INFORM: stress CMR vs FFR for management of stable chest pain. JACC 2019 Knuuti J et al. ESC guidelines for chronic coronary syndromes. Eur Heart J 2020 Kim RJ et al. Transmurality of LGE and functional recovery. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2013
Valve regurgitation — CMR quantification methods

CMR is the reference standard for quantifying valvular regurgitation. Unlike echocardiography, CMR provides direct volumetric measurement independent of geometric assumptions or acoustic windows. Multiple methods exist, and the choice depends on the valve involved and the clinical question. Using more than one method and cross-checking internal consistency is good practice.

Aortic regurgitation (AR)
MethodCalculationNotes
Direct phase-contrastRegV = reverse flow on AV phase-contrastMost direct method. Place slice just above aortic valve tips. Ensure VENC is appropriate. Gives RegV and RegF directly from the flow curve.
Volumetric (LV SV − AV forward flow)RegV = LV SV − AV forward flowCross-check method. LV SV from cine contouring. Forward flow from phase-contrast. Discrepancy = regurgitant volume. Requires accurate LV contouring.
Volumetric (LV SV − RV SV)RegV = LV SV − RV SVOnly valid if AR is the sole significant valve lesion and no intracardiac shunt. RV SV acts as internal control for true forward cardiac output.
Descending aorta flow reversalQualitative / semi-quantitativeHolodiastolic flow reversal in descending aorta indicates severe AR. Supportive sign — not a standalone quantification method.
  • AR severity: RegF <15% = mild; 15–33% = moderate; ≥33% = severe (surgical threshold)
  • Cross-check: if LV SV − AV forward flow ≠ phase-contrast reverse flow, investigate source of error
Mitral regurgitation (MR)
MethodCalculationNotes
Indirect volumetric (preferred)MR RegV = LV SV − AV forward flowThe standard CMR method for MR. LV SV from cine contouring minus aortic forward flow from phase-contrast. The difference is the MR volume.
Biventricular comparisonMR RegV = LV SV − RV SVOnly valid if no other significant valve lesion and no shunt. Useful as a cross-check if AV flow quality is suboptimal.
Direct MV flow (4D flow)MR RegV = MV inflow − AV forward flowRequires 4D flow CMR — not routine. Directly measures mitral inflow including regurgitant component. Research tool primarily.
  • MR severity: RegF <20% = mild; 20–40% = moderate; ≥40% = severe
  • No direct phase-contrast method for MR (valve moves too much) — always use indirect volumetric approach
  • Functional MR: always report with LVEDVI and LVEF — guides decision between surgery and medical therapy
Pulmonary regurgitation (PR)
MethodCalculationNotes
Direct phase-contrast (preferred)PR RegV = reverse flow on MPA phase-contrastThe standard method. Place slice just above pulmonary valve. Most commonly used in repaired tetralogy of Fallot (ToF). Gives RegV and RegF directly.
Volumetric (RV SV − MPA forward flow)PR RegV = RV SV − MPA forward flowCross-check method. Requires accurate RV contouring (use transaxial stack for reproducibility).
Volumetric (RV SV − LV SV)PR RegV = RV SV − LV SVOnly valid if PR is the sole significant valve lesion and no intracardiac shunt.
  • PR severity: RegF <20% = mild; 20–40% = moderate; ≥40% = severe
  • PVR thresholds in repaired ToF: consider PVR if PR RegF ≥40% AND RVEDVI >160 mL/m² (or >150 with symptoms)
  • Severe PR jet is laminar and may be invisible on cine — phase-contrast is essential for quantification
Tricuspid regurgitation (TR)
MethodCalculationNotes
Indirect volumetricTR RegV = RV SV − MPA forward flowSame principle as MR quantification but for the right side. RV SV from contouring minus pulmonary forward flow.
Biventricular comparisonTR RegV = RV SV − LV SVOnly valid if no other significant valve lesion and no shunt.
  • TR severity: RegF <20% = mild; 20–40% = moderate; ≥40% = severe
  • TR is often functional — always report RV volumes and function alongside TR severity
Internal consistency checks
CheckExpected resultIf discrepant
LV SV vs RV SV (no valve disease, no shunt)Should be equal (within 10 mL or 10%)Suggests contouring error, valve disease, or intracardiac shunt
AV forward flow vs MPA forward flow (no shunt)Should be equalSuggests AV or PV regurgitation, or shunt (Qp:Qs ≠ 1)
LV SV − AV forward flow vs phase-contrast reverse flowShould match for AR quantificationSuggests contouring error or through-plane motion affecting flow measurement
Multiple valve lesionsCannot use biventricular comparisonMust use direct or indirect methods per valve — biventricular SV comparison only valid with a single regurgitant lesion
References
Schulz-Menger J et al. SCMR standardised post-processing. JCMR 2020 Lancellotti P et al. EACVI recommendations for valve regurgitation assessment. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging 2018 Myerson SG. CMR for valvular heart disease. JACC Cardiovasc Imaging 2021
eGFR (CKD-EPI) & gadolinium safety
Gadolinium dose calculator
Extracellular volume fraction (ECV)

ECV = (1 − Hct) × (ΔR1 myocardium / ΔR1 blood), where ΔR1 = (1/post-contrast T1) − (1/native T1). Haematocrit must be measured within 24h of the CMR study.

T2* myocardial iron (thalassaemia)
Ejection fraction & cardiac output
BSA (DuBois & DuBois)
Qp:Qs shunt calculator

Qp = PV forward flow · Qs = AV forward flow · Qp:Qs >1.5 = haemodynamically significant shunt

Regurgitant fraction calculator

Two methods are available. The biventricular SV method compares LV SV with RV SV — valid only with a single regurgitant lesion and no shunt. The flow method compares ventricular SV with phase-contrast forward flow (indirect), or uses reverse flow directly. Using both and cross-checking is recommended.

Method 1 — Biventricular SV method

RegV = LV SV − RV SV. Only valid if AR is the sole significant valve lesion and no intracardiac shunt.

Method 2 — Flow method

Indirect: RegV = LV SV − AV forward flow. Direct (AR/PR only): RegV = reverse flow from phase-contrast curve.

Atrial size — indexed 4-chamber area

Measure maximal LA and RA area from the 4-chamber cine view at ventricular end-systole (just before AV valve opening). Index to BSA. Reference: Kawel-Boehm N et al, JCMR 2020 (Table 5).

Mass ID
Cardiac mass identification

Answer each question to narrow the differential. The tree works through patient age, comorbidities, mass location, size, morphology, and CMR tissue characteristics in sequence.
